Ayahs

الٓمٓ (١)
Alif, Lam, Meem. (1)
غُلِبَتِ ٱلرُّومُ (٢)
The Byzantines has been vanquished (2)
فِىٓ أَدْنَى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَهُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ غَلَبِهِمْ سَيَغْلِبُونَ (٣)
in a nearby land, but they, after their defeat, will be victorious (3)
فِى بِضْعِ سِنِينَ ۗ لِلَّهِ ٱلْأَمْرُ مِن قَبْلُ وَمِنۢ بَعْدُ ۚ وَيَوْمَئِذٍ يَفْرَحُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ (٤)
in a few years. All command belongs to Allah, before this and hereafter, and on that day the faithful will rejoice (4)
بِنَصْرِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ يَنصُرُ مَن يَشَآءُ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (٥)
at Allah’s help. He helps whomever He wishes, and He is the All-mighty, the All-merciful. (5)
وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ لَا يُخْلِفُ ٱللَّهُ وَعْدَهُۥ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٦)
[This is] a promise of Allah: Allah does not break His promise, but most people do not know. (6)
يَعْلَمُونَ ظَـٰهِرًا مِّنَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَهُمْ عَنِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمْ غَـٰفِلُونَ (٧)
They know just an outward aspect of the life of the world, but they are oblivious of the Hereafter. (7)

Content of Surah Ar-Rum

Since this Surah, according to the well-known view, was revealed entirely in Makkah, the content and spirit of the Makkan Surahs are reflected within it. That is, above all else, it discusses the issues of the "Origin" (Creation) and the "Resurrection" (Ma'ad). This is because the Makkan period was a time for teaching fundamental Islamic beliefs, Monotheism (Tawhid), struggling against polytheism, and drawing attention to the Hereafter and the Court of Resurrection. Amidst these discussions, other related topics are also presented.

In fact, the contents of this Surah can be summarized into seven sections:

  1. The prediction of the victory of the Romans over the Persians in a war that would break out in the future, occasioned by a conversation that took place between the Muslims and the polytheists in this regard, the details of which will come, Allah willing.

  2. A glimpse into the mindset and state of faithless individuals, followed by threats against them regarding Divine punishment and retribution on the Day of Resurrection.

  3. A significant portion of verses regarding the greatness of Allah in the heavens and the earth and within human beings—such as the emergence of life from death and death from life, the creation of man from dust, the system of pair-bonding and the creation of spouses for humans and the relationship of affection between them, the creation of the heavens and the earth, the diversity of languages, the blessing of sleep at night and movement during the day, the appearance of thunder, lightning, and rain, the life of the earth after its death, and the governance of the affairs of the heavens and the earth by the command of Allah.

  4. Discussion of primordial Monotheism (Tawhid al-Fitri) after explaining the horizons-based and soul-based (external and internal) proofs for knowing God.

  5. Returning to the description and clarification of the states of faithless and sinful individuals, and the appearance of corruption on earth due to their sins.

  6. A reference to the issue of ownership, the rights of kinsmen, and the condemnation of usury (Riba).

  7. A renewed return to the proofs of Monotheism, the signs of Truth, and matters related to the Resurrection.

Altogether, in this Surah, as in other Surahs of the Quran, argumentative, emotional, and rhetorical issues are so intertwined that they provide a complete remedy for the guidance and education of souls.

The Virtue of Surah Ar-Rum

In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, which we have previously mentioned, it is stated:

"Whoever recites Surah al-Ankabut and Ar-Rum in the month of Ramadan on the twenty-third night, by Allah, he is among the people of Paradise, and I make no exception in this statement... and these two Surahs have an important status before the Lord." [^1]

And in another Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated:

"Whoever recites Surah Ar-Rum, he will have a reward equal to ten good deeds multiplied by the number of every angel that glorifies Allah between the heavens and the earth, and he will compensate for what he missed during his day and night." [^2]

It is obvious that one who embeds the content of this Surah—which is entirely a lesson in the Monotheism of Allah and the great Court of Resurrection—into his soul and spirit, perceives the watchfulness of the Lord at every moment, and considers the Day of Judgment and the Court of Divine Justice a certainty, will have his heart so filled with Divine piety (Taqwa) that he becomes worthy of such a great reward.

[^1]: Thawab al-A'mal by Saduq, as quoted in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 169. [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, beginning of Surah Ar-Rum.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ الم (1) غُلِبَتِ الرُّومُ (2) فِي أَدْنَى الْأَرْضِ وَ هُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ غَلَبِهِمْ سَيَغْلِبُونَ (3) فِي بِضْعِ سِنِينَ لِلَّهِ الْأَمْرُ مِنْ قَبْلُ وَ مِنْ بَعْدُ وَ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَفْرَحُ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ (4) بِنَصْرِ اللَّهِ يَنْصُرُ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَ هُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ (5) وَعْدَ اللَّهِ لا يُخْلِفُ اللَّهُ وَعْدَهُ وَ لكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَعْلَمُونَ (6) يَعْلَمُونَ ظاهِراً مِنَ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَ هُمْ عَنِ الْآخِرَةِ هُمْ غافِلُونَ (7)

Occasion of Revelation

The great commentators all agree that the opening verses of this Surah were revealed because while the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was in Makkah and the believers were in the minority, a war broke out between the Persians and the Romans, and in this battle, the Persians were victorious.

The polytheists of Makkah took this as a good omen and considered it evidence for the validity of their polytheism! They said: "The Persians are Magians and polytheists (dualists), while the Romans are Christians and People of the Book. Just as the 'Persians' overcame the 'Romans,' the final victory belongs to polytheism, and the scroll of Islam will soon be rolled up, and we shall be victorious."

Although such conclusions had no real basis or substance, in that atmosphere and environment, they were not without impact for propaganda among the ignorant people; therefore, this matter weighed heavily upon the Muslims.

The above verses were revealed and stated decisively: Although the Persians were victorious in this battle, it will not be long before they are defeated by the Romans. It even specified the timeframe for this prediction, stating that this matter would occur within just a few years!

This decisive prediction of the Quran—which on one hand is a sign of the miracle of this Heavenly Book and its bringer's reliance on the infinite knowledge of the Lord regarding the Unseen, and on the other hand was the opposite of the polytheists' omen—so encouraged the Muslims that it is even said some of them made a significant bet with the polytheists on this matter! (At that time, the ruling prohibiting such betting had not yet been revealed). [^1]

Commentary

An Extraordinary Prediction!

This Surah is among the 29 Surahs that begin with "Disconnected Letters" (Alif, Lam, Mim).

We have repeatedly discussed the interpretation of these Disconnected Letters (especially at the beginning of Surah al-Baqarah, Al-Imran, and al-A'raf).

The only thing that draws attention here is that, unlike many Surahs that begin with Disconnected Letters followed by a discussion of the greatness of the Quran, we do not find a discussion of the Quran's greatness here. Instead, there is talk of the defeat of the Romans and their renewed victory in the future. However, upon closer inspection, it becomes clear that this discussion is also an expression of the Quran's greatness, as this news of the Unseen regarding the future is considered one of the signs of the miracle and greatness of this Heavenly Book.

After mentioning the Disconnected Letters, He says: "The Romans have been defeated" (غُلِبَتِ الرُّومُ). "And this defeat occurred in a nearby land" (فِي أَدْنَى الْأَرْضِ).

Near to you, the people of Makkah, in the north of the Arabian Peninsula, in the lands of Sham (Greater Syria), in a region between "Busra" and "Adhru'at."

From this, it becomes clear that "Rome" refers to the Eastern Roman Empire (Byzantine), not the Western Roman Empire.

Some commentators, such as the late Sheikh Tusi in Al-Tibyan, have suggested the possibility that it refers to a point near the land of the Persians—meaning it occurred in a place that was the closest point between Persia and Rome. [^1] While it is true that the first interpretation is more consistent with the definite article "Al" (of familiarity) in the word "Al-Ard" (the land), for several reasons, the second interpretation seems more accurate, as we shall explain.

There is also a third interpretation here, which perhaps does not differ much in result from the second: that "this land" refers to the land of the Romans themselves, meaning they suffered defeat at their closest borders with Persia. This points to the significance and depth of this defeat, because a defeat at distant points and remote borders is not so significant; what is significant is when a country suffers defeat at its closest borders to an enemy that is stronger and more powerful than anywhere else.

Therefore, the mention of the phrase "فِي أَدْنَى الْأَرْضِ" (in the nearest land) is an indication of the significance of this defeat, and naturally, the prediction of the victory of the defeated party within the next few years becomes even more significant, as it cannot be predicted except through a miracle!

Then He adds: "And they (the Romans), after their defeat, will soon be victorious" (وَ هُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ غَلَبِهِمْ سَيَغْلِبُونَ).

Even though the word "سَيَغْلِبُونَ" (they will soon be victorious) was sufficient to convey the meaning, the expression "مِنْ بَعْدِ غَلَبِهِمْ" (after their being overcome) was specifically added to make the significance of this victory more apparent. This is because for a defeated population to become victorious, especially at their closest and strongest borders within a short period, is unexpected, and the Quran explicitly reports this unexpected event.

Then He specifies the range of years with this phrase: "In a few years!" (فِي بِضْعِ سِنِينَ). And we know that the concept of the term "Bidh'" is a minimum of three and a maximum of nine. [^1] And if you see that Allah informs of the future, it is because "all things and all affairs are in His hands, both before the victory and after the victory of this defeated people" (لِلَّهِ الْأَمْرُ مِنْ قَبْلُ وَ مِنْ بَعْدُ).

It is obvious that everything being in the hand of Allah and by His command and will does not prevent our choice, free will, effort, struggle, and Jihad in the path of intended goals. In other words, this expression does not seek to negate the free will of others, but rather seeks to clarify the point that He is the Powerful by Essence and the Absolute Owner, and whoever has anything, has it from Him.

[^1]: This occasion of revelation has been mentioned with various expressions in Majma' al-Bayan, Al-Mizan, Nur al-Thaqalayn, Abu al-Futuh Razi, Tafsir Fakhr Razi, Tafsir Alusi, Tafsir Fi Zilal, and other commentaries. [^1]: Tafsir al-Tibyan, Vol. 8, p. 206. [^1]: Regarding the meaning of "Bidh'," other possibilities have also been mentioned, including that its minimum is three and its maximum is ten, or its minimum is one and its maximum is nine, or its minimum is six and its maximum is nine; what we have stated is the most well-known.

Then He adds that if today, when the Romans were defeated, the polytheists rejoiced, "on that day when the Romans are victorious, the believers will rejoice!" (وَ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَفْرَحُ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ).

Yes, "they will rejoice in the victory of Allah" (بِنَصْرِ اللَّهِ). "Allah helps whom He wills, and He is the All-Mighty, the Most Merciful" (يَنْصُرُ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَ هُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ).

Regarding what is meant by the joy of the Muslims on that day, a group has said it refers to joy over the victory of the Romans. Even though they were also in the ranks of the disbelievers, because they possessed a Heavenly Book, their victory over the polytheistic Magians was a stage of the victory of "Monotheism" over "Polytheism."

Some have added: The believers rejoiced because they took this event as a good omen and a proof of their own victory over the polytheists.

Or, their joy was because the greatness of the Quran and the truth of its decisive prediction—which itself was considered an important spiritual victory for the Muslims—became manifest on that day.

It also does not seem unlikely that the victory of the Romans coincided with one of the victories of the Muslims over the polytheists, especially since it is mentioned in some words of the great commentators that this victory coincided with the victory of "Badr," or coincided with the "Peace of Hudaybiyyah," which was also considered a great victory in its own right. Specifically, the expression "بِنَصْرِ اللَّهِ" (by the help of Allah) is also consistent with this meaning.

In short, the Muslims rejoiced on that day for several reasons: - From the victory of the People of the Book over the Magians, which was a scene of the triumph of God-worship over polytheism. - From the spiritual victory due to the manifestation of the Quran's miracle. - And from the coinciding victory, which was likely the "Peace of Hudaybiyyah" or another of the Muslims' conquests.

Again, for further emphasis, He says: "This is a promise Allah has made" (وَعْدَ اللَّهِ) [^1] "and Allah never fails in His promise, though most people do not know" (لا يُخْلِفُ اللَّهُ وَعْدَهُ وَ لكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَعْلَمُونَ).

The reason for the people's lack of awareness is their lack of knowledge regarding Allah and His knowledge and power. They have not recognized Allah properly, and therefore they are not correctly aware of the fact that it is impossible for Him to fail in His promise. This is because failing a promise is either due to ignorance—where a matter was hidden and then became manifest, causing a change of mind—or due to weakness and inability, where the promiser has not changed his mind but lacks the power to fulfill his promise.

However, the God who is both aware of the consequences of all affairs and whose power is above all powers will never fail in His promise.

Then He adds: "These short-sighted people only see the outward appearance of the life of this world and are heedless of the Hereafter and the end of affairs!" (يَعْلَمُونَ ظاهِراً مِنَ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَ هُمْ عَنِ الْآخِرَةِ هُمْ غافِلُونَ).

[^1]: "Wa'd Allah" is in the accusative case as an "absolute object" (Maf'ul Mutlaq), and its governing verb is omitted, understood from the preceding sentence "Sayaghlibun," which is the instance of the Divine promise. It is estimated as: Wa'ada Allahu dhalika wa'dan.

They are only aware of the life of this world, and even of this life, they have settled for the outward appearance. A collection of pastimes, fleeting pleasures, dreams, and imaginations constitutes their perception of worldly life. The arrogance and heedlessness inherent in this perception are hidden from no one.

If they also knew the inner reality and depth of this worldly life, it would have been sufficient for them to recognize the Hereafter. This is because careful attention to this fleeting life shows that it is a link in a long chain and a stage of a great journey, just as careful attention to life in the embryonic stage shows that the ultimate goal is not that life itself, but rather it is a preliminary stage for an expansive life.

Yes, they only see the outward appearance of this life and are heedless of its content, meanings, and hidden realities.

Interestingly, by repeating the pronoun "Hum" (they), He points to the fact that they themselves are the cause of this heedlessness and ignorance. It is just like someone saying to us: "You made me heedless of this matter," and we reply: "You yourself became heedless," meaning you were the cause of your own heedlessness.

Key Points

1. The Miracle of the Quran from the Perspective of Knowledge of the Unseen

One of the ways to prove the miracle of the Quran is its reports of the Unseen, a clear example of which is found in these verses under discussion. Over several verses, with various types of emphasis, it informs of the great victory of a defeated army a few years later and introduces it as an infallible Divine promise.

  • On one hand, it informs of the principle of victory (وَ هُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ غَلَبِهِمْ سَيَغْلِبُونَ).
  • On the other hand, it informs of another victory for the Muslims over the disbelievers coinciding with that same time (وَ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَفْرَحُ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ بِنَصْرِ اللَّهِ).
  • On a third hand, it explicitly states that this matter will occur within a few years (فِي بِضْعِ سِنِينَ).
  • And on a fourth hand, with two-fold emphasis, it confirms the certainty of this Divine promise (وَعْدَ اللَّهِ - لا يُخْلِفُ اللَّهُ وَعْدَهُ).

History tells us that before nine years had passed, these two events took place. The Romans were victorious over the Persians in a new battle, and coinciding with that time, the Muslims achieved a striking victory over their enemies with the Peace of Hudaybiyyah (and according to one narration, in the Battle of Badr).

Now the question arises: Can a human being with ordinary knowledge report so decisively on an event of such importance? Even if it were politically predictable—which it was not—it would have to be said with caution and mention of probability, not with such clarity and decisiveness that if its failure became apparent, it would provide the best evidence for the enemies to invalidate the claim of prophethood.

The truth is that matters such as predicting the victory of a great country like Rome, along with the issue of Mubahalah (Mutual Cursing), clearly show that the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله had his heart set elsewhere and had support from another source. Otherwise, no one under normal circumstances has the ability or courage for such things.

Especially since a study of the Prophet of Islam's state shows that he was not one to act recklessly; rather, his actions were calculated. Such a claim from such a person shows that he relied on the supernatural world, on Divine revelation, and the infinite knowledge of Allah.

We will soon discuss the historical application of this Quranic prediction.

2. The Outward-Lookers!

Fundamentally, the vision of a believing and godly person differs greatly from that of a materialist or a polytheist.

The former, according to the monotheistic belief, considers the world to be the creation of a Wise and Aware God whose every action is based on calculation and a plan. For this reason, he believes the world is a collection of secrets and precise mysteries; nothing in this world is simple; all the words of this content-rich and meaningful book are significant.

This monotheistic vision tells him: Do not pass by any event or any subject simply, for the simplest matters may be the most complex.

He always looks at the depth of this world and is not satisfied with its appearances. He has learned this lesson in the school of Monotheism. He attributes a great purpose to the universe and sees everything within the circle of that purpose.

In contrast, a materialist and faithless individual considers the world a collection of blind, deaf, and purposeless events, and thinks of nothing but its outward appearance. He does not attribute any depth or inner reality to it at all. How can one attribute importance and depth to a book whose collection of lines is the same as what a child produces with purposeless hand movements on paper?!

Even according to some great scientists of the natural sciences, all human thinkers from every stratum and group who have risen to contemplate the order of the universe have possessed a type of religious thinking (pay attention).

Einstein, the famous contemporary scientist, says: "It is difficult to find anyone among the thinking brains of the world who does not possess a kind of religious feeling peculiar to himself... This religion differs from the religion of an ordinary person... The religion of this scientist is a rapturous amazement at the extraordinary and precise order of the universe, which occasionally pulls back the veil from secrets compared to which all human systematic efforts and thoughts are but a negligible reflection!" [^1]

Elsewhere he says: "The very thing that caused scientists, thinkers, and discoverers throughout the centuries and ages to study the precise secrets of the universe in the corner of solitude was this religious belief of theirs." [^2]

On the other hand, how is it possible for someone who considers this world the final stage and the main goal to see it the same as someone who considers it a "farm" and a "field of testing" for the eternal life that follows it? The former sees nothing but its outward appearance, while the latter contemplates its profound depths.

And this difference in vision affects their entire lives: - That "outward-looker" considers charity (Infaq) a cause of loss and harm, while this "monotheist" considers it a highly profitable trade. - One considers usury a means of increasing income, and the other a cause of burden, misery, and loss. - One considers Jihad a cause of trouble and martyrdom to mean destruction, while the other considers Jihad the secret of honor and martyrdom to be eternal life.

Yes, faithless individuals see only an outward appearance of the life of this world and are heedless of the Hereafter (يَعْلَمُونَ ظاهِراً مِنَ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَ هُمْ عَنِ الْآخِرَةِ هُمْ غافِلُونَ).

[^1]: and [^2]: Quoted from the book The World as I See It.

3. Historical Application

To know at what historical juncture the defeat of the Romans by the Persians and then their victory occurred, it is sufficient to know that in today's histories, during the era of Khosrow Parviz, we encounter a period of long war between the Persians and the Romans that lasted twenty-four years, from 604 AD to 628 AD.

Around the year 616 AD, two famous Persian generals named "Shahrbaraz" and "Shahin" attacked the territory of the Eastern Roman Empire, gave them a severe defeat, and raided the regions of Sham, Egypt, and Asia Minor. The Eastern Roman government, which had suffered a crushing defeat, went to the brink of extinction, and the Persian government conquered all its Asian possessions along with Egypt.

And this was around the seventh year of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله mission (Bi'thah).

However, the Roman Emperor "Heraclius" began his offensive against Persia from the year 622 AD and inflicted successive defeats upon the army of Khosrow Parviz. These wars, which were in favor of the Romans, continued until 628 AD. Khosrow Parviz suffered a severe defeat, and the people of Persia deposed him from the throne and placed his son "Shiroe" (Kavad II) in his place.

Considering that the birth of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله occurred in 571 AD and the mission of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله occurred in 610 AD, the defeat of the Romans corresponds to the seventh year of the mission, and the end of the Roman victory and the defeat of Khosrow Parviz corresponds to the fifth to sixth year of the Hijrah. We know that in the fifth year, the conquest of the Trench (Khandaq) took place, and in the sixth year, the Peace of Hudaybiyyah occurred. Of course, the transmission of news of the Perso-Roman war to the region of Hijaz and Makkah also usually occupied some time, and in this way, the historical application of this Quranic report becomes clear (pay attention).

Ayahs

أَوَلَمْ يَتَفَكَّرُوا۟ فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِم ۗ مَّا خَلَقَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَآ إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ وَأَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى ۗ وَإِنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ ٱلنَّاسِ بِلِقَآئِ رَبِّهِمْ لَكَـٰفِرُونَ (٨)
Have they not reflected in their own souls? Allah did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them except with consummate wisdom and for a specified term. Indeed many people disbelieve in the encounter with their Lord. (8)
أَوَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَيَنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ كَانُوٓا۟ أَشَدَّ مِنْهُمْ قُوَّةً وَأَثَارُوا۟ ٱلْأَرْضَ وَعَمَرُوهَآ أَكْثَرَ مِمَّا عَمَرُوهَا وَجَآءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ ۖ فَمَا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ لِيَظْلِمَهُمْ وَلَـٰكِن كَانُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ (٩)
Have they not travelled in the land and seen how was the fate of those who were before them? They were more powerful than them, and they plowed the earth and developed it more than they have developed it. Their apostles brought them manifest proofs. So it was not Allah who wronged them, but it was they who used to wrong themselves. (9)
ثُمَّ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَسَـٰٓـُٔوا۟ ٱلسُّوٓأَىٰٓ أَن كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَكَانُوا۟ بِهَا يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (١٠)
Then the fate of those who committed misdeeds was that they denied the signs of Allah and they used to deride them. (10)

Commentary

The Fate of the Evildoers

In the final verse of the previous discussion, the talk was about those who are superficial—those whose intellectual horizon is limited to the boundaries of this world and the material realm, and who are unaware of the Resurrection and the metaphysical world.

In the verses under discussion and the upcoming verses, various topics regarding the Origin (Creation) and the Return (Resurrection) are addressed. First, in the form of a reproachful interrogation, it says: "Do they not reflect within themselves that Allah did not create the heavens and the earth and what is between them except with truth and for an appointed term?" (أَ وَ لَمْ يَتَفَكَّرُوا فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ ما خَلَقَ اللَّهُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ وَ ما بَيْنَهُما إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَ أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى).

This means that if they were to think deeply and refer to their own consciences and the judgment of their intellect, they would clearly realize two things: First, that the world was created based on truth, and systems govern it that testify to the existence of perfect intellect and power in the Creator of this world.

Secondly, this world is heading toward decline and annihilation. Since a Wise Creator could not have created it in vain, it is proof that another world, which is the abode of permanence, exists after this world. Otherwise, the creation of this world would have no meaning, and this vast and extensive creation would be pointless for just these few days of worldly life. From this, they can deduce the existence of the Hereafter.

Therefore, contemplating the order and truthfulness of this world is evidence of the Origin, and contemplating its "appointed term" is evidence of the Resurrection (reflect on this).

Thus, at the end of the verse, it adds: "And indeed, many of the people are disbelievers in the meeting with their Lord" (وَ إِنَّ كَثِيراً مِنَ النَّاسِ بِلِقاءِ رَبِّهِمْ لَكافِرُونَ).

Either they deny the Resurrection altogether—as frequently quoted in Quranic verses from the polytheists who said: "When we have become dust, shall we truly be brought to life again? This is a strange thing! This is impossible! This is proof of the speaker's madness!" (Surah Ar-Ra'd, Verse 5; Surah Al-Mu'minun, Verse 35; Surah An-Naml, Verse 67; and Surah Qaf, Verse 3).

Or, they do not deny it with their tongues, but their actions are so tainted and shameful that it shows they have no belief in the Resurrection; for if they believed, they should not be so corrupt and corrupting.

Furthermore, the expression "فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ" (within themselves) does not mean they should study the secrets of their own existence, as Fakhr al-Razi stated in his commentary; rather, it means they should reflect within their own souls, through intellect and conscience, upon the creation of the heavens and the earth.

The expression "بالحق" (with truth) may have two meanings: one is that the creation is coupled with truth, law, and order; the other is that the purpose of creation was a truthful purpose. Of course, these two interpretations are not contradictory [1]. The expression "لقاء ربهم" (meeting their Lord), as we have said many times, refers to the Day of Judgment and the Resurrection where veils are removed and man recognizes God in His majesty through inner intuition.

[1]: In the first case, the "Baa" in "بالحق" is for accompaniment (musahabah), and in the second case, it carries the meaning of "Laam" (for/purpose).

Since the expression "appointed term" expresses the reality that the life of this world does not last forever—and this is a warning to all world-worshippers—the next verse adds: "Have they not traveled through the earth and observed how was the end of those before them?" (أَ وَ لَمْ يَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَيَنْظُرُوا كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ).

"They were superior to them in power, and they turned up the earth and inhabited it more than these have inhabited it" (كانُوا أَشَدَّ مِنْهُمْ قُوَّةً وَ أَثارُوا الْأَرْضَ وَ عَمَرُوها أَكْثَرَ مِمَّا عَمَرُوها).

"And their messengers came to them with clear proofs" (و جاءتهم رسلهم بالبينات).

But they acted with stubbornness, did not submit to the truth, and were afflicted with painful divine punishment.

"Allah never wronged them, but they used to wrong themselves" (فَما كانَ اللَّهُ لِيَظْلِمَهُمْ وَ لكِنْ كانُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ).

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 16, p. 373

In fact, the Quran refers to nations that possessed a striking superiority over the polytheists contemporary to the Prophet in terms of physical strength and financial power, and it sets their painful fate as a lesson for them.

The phrase "أَثارُوا الْأَرْضَ" (they turned up the earth) may refer to plowing the land for agriculture and planting trees, or digging canals and qanats, or excavating the foundations of great buildings, or all of these; because the phrase "أثاروا الأرض" has a broad meaning that includes all these matters which are precursors to development and prosperity [1]. Since in the world of that time, the greatest power was in the hands of those who were more advanced in agriculture and had made significant progress in constructing buildings, it clarifies the superiority of these nations over the polytheists of Mecca, whose power in this regard was very limited.

Yet, despite all this capability, when they denied the divine signs and rejected the messengers, they had no power to escape the grip of punishment. How then can you escape?! These painful punishments were the product of their own deeds and the fruit of their own hands; it was they who wronged themselves, and Allah never permits any wrong or injustice toward anyone.

[1]: "أثار" is from the root "ثور" (rhyming with ghawr) meaning to scatter or stir up. The reason Arabs call an ox "ثور" is because the earth is plowed with it.

The final verse under discussion describes the ultimate stage of their disbelief and says: "Then the end of those who did evil was that they denied the signs of Allah and used to mock them" (ثُمَّ كانَ عاقِبَةَ الَّذِينَ أَساؤُا السُّواى‌ أَنْ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِ اللَّهِ وَ كانُوا بِها يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ).

Yes, sin and pollution fall upon the human soul like leprosy, eating away and destroying the spirit of faith. It reaches a point where it leads to the denial of divine signs, and goes even further, driving one to ridicule and mock the prophets and divine verses. It reaches a stage where no sermon, advice, or warning is effective anymore, and no path remains except the whip of painful divine punishment!

A look at the pages of history regarding many rebels and criminals shows that they were not like this at the beginning; at least a faint light of faith shone in their hearts. However, committing successive sins caused them to distance themselves from faith and piety day by day, until they finally reached the ultimate stage of disbelief.

In the famous sermon of Lady Zainab (peace be upon her), the lioness of Karbala, which she delivered in Damascus in front of Yazid, this verse appears with the same interpretation mentioned above.

For when she saw that Yazid was mocking everything by uttering blasphemous words and the famous verses "The Hashimites played with the kingdom..." which expressed his lack of faith in the foundation of Islam, after praising Allah and sending blessings upon the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), she said:

"Allah spoke the truth when He said: 'Then the end of those who did evil was that they denied the signs of Allah and used to mock them'..."

Meaning: If today you deny Islam and faith with these blasphemous poems and say to your polytheist ancestors who were killed by Muslims in the Battle of Badr, "I wish you were here to see my revenge against the Banu Hashim," it is no surprise. This is exactly what Allah said—that criminals eventually deny Our signs... and she spoke extensively on this matter (for more details, refer to Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 45, p. 157) [1].

[1]: According to what we said in the commentary of the verse, "السوأى" is the object (maf'ul) of "أساؤا", and the phrase "أن كذبوا بآيات الله" stands in place of the noun (ism) of "كان", while its predicate (khabar) is "عاقبة". The late Allamah Tabataba'i in Al-Mizan mentioned this as a possibility, though he did not choose it himself, and Abu al-Baqa in the book Imla' ma Manna bihi al-Rahman (p. 185, Vol. 2) brought it as one of two acceptable possibilities. However, most commentators (such as Tabarsi, Al-Mizan, Fakhr al-Razi, Alusi, Abu al-Futuh Razi, Qurtubi, Fi Zilal, and Tibyan) favored another possibility in interpreting the verse: that "سوء" (evil/bad) was the noun of kana and "أن كذبوا" is in the position of providing the reason (ta'lil). According to this interpretation, the meaning of the verse becomes: "The end of the matter for those who did evil deeds was evil, because they denied Our signs" (similar to "For those who did good is the best [reward]").

But in fairness, this interpretation is contrary to what is understood from the apparent meaning of the verse, and its selection by this group of commentators cannot divert us from what is consistent with the apparent text, especially since they are forced to assume the word "Laam" (because) is implied before the phrase "أن كذبوا", and assuming implied words is contrary to the apparent meaning (reflect on this).

But as for those who were faithless and denied Our signs and the encounter of the Hereafter, they will be brought to the punishment. (16)

Commentary

The Fate of the Criminals on the Day of Resurrection

In the preceding verses, mention was made of the deniers who took the signs of Allah in mockery. In the verses under discussion, by describing a portion of the topics regarding the Hereafter and the fate of the criminals on the Day of Resurrection, the discussions previously mentioned about the Resurrection in the earlier verses are completed.

First, He says: "Allah originates the creation, then He repeats it, then to Him you will be returned" (اللَّهُ يَبْدَؤُا الْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُ ثُمَّ إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ).

In this verse, a short and meaningful argument for the issue of the Resurrection is stated, which has been repeated in other verses of the Quran with different expressions. That argument is: "The same One who had the power for the first creation also has the power for the Resurrection, and the law of justice as well as the wisdom of Allah necessitates that this repeated creation takes place."

The phrase "then to Him you will be returned" (ثُمَّ إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ) refers to the fact that after the life of the Resurrection, all of you will return to the court of the Lord and His reward and recompense. Beyond this, the believers who are on the path of divine perfection will continue their evolution toward infinity and toward the Pure Essence of the Lord.

The next verse depicts the state of the criminals as such: "And the Day the Hour is established, the criminals will be plunged into despair and grief" (وَ يَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ يُبْلِسُ الْمُجْرِمُونَ).

"Yublisu" (يبلس) is derived from the root "Iblas" (ابلاس), which originally means the grief and sorrow that arises from intense hopelessness and despair.

It is obvious that if the thing a person despairs of is not a necessity, his despair is not significant. However, grief and sorrow indicate that in these instances, he has despaired of a necessary matter; therefore, some commentators consider "necessity" to be part of the meaning of the root "Iblas."

"Iblis" (Satan) was also named Iblis for this reason—that he became despaired of Allah's mercy and was grieved.

In any case, the criminals have the right to be despaired and grieved on that day, for they have brought neither faith nor righteous deeds to the field of Gathering, they have no helper or supporter, and there is no possibility of returning to the world to compensate for the past.

Therefore, in the next verse, He adds: "And they will have no intercessors from among their 'partners'" (وَ لَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُمْ مِنْ شُرَكائِهِمْ شُفَعاءُ). These are the same idols and fabricated deities that, whenever they were asked why they worshipped them, they would say: "These are our intercessors with Allah" (هؤُلاءِ شُفَعاؤُنا عِنْدَ اللَّهِ) (Surah Yunus, Verse 18).

There, they will realize that no benefit results from the existence of these hollow and worthless deities.

For this reason, "they will become disbelievers in the partners they associated with Allah, and they will express hatred and disavowal toward them" (وَ كانُوا بِشُرَكائِهِمْ كافِرِينَ).

Why should they not disbelieve? While they see that these deities not only fail to solve any of their problems but, according to the Quran, they rise to deny them and say: "O Lord! They did not worship us" (ما كانُوا إِيَّانا يَعْبُدُونَ) (rather, they were worshipping their own carnal desires) (Surah Al-Qasas, Verse 63).

And even more than this, the deities will gird themselves with enmity against them, as we read in Surah Al-Ahqaf, Verse 6: "And when the people are gathered, they [the deities] will be enemies to them and will deny their worship" (وَ إِذا حُشِرَ النَّاسُ كانُوا لَهُمْ أَعْداءً وَ كانُوا بِعِبادَتِهِمْ كافِرِينَ).

Then, referring to the different groups of people on that day, He says: "And the Day the Hour is established, on that Day they shall be separated" (وَ يَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَتَفَرَّقُونَ).

"As for those who believed and did righteous deeds, they shall be in a garden of Paradise, delighted and joyful, such that the signs of joy will be manifest on their faces" (فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ فَهُمْ فِي رَوْضَةٍ يُحْبَرُونَ). "Yuhbarun" (يحبرون) is derived from the root "Habr" (حبر), meaning a beautiful and good effect. It is also used for a state of joy and delight whose signs appear on the face. Since the hearts of the people of Paradise are so overflowing with joy and delight that its signs appear in their entire being, this expression is used for them.

"Rawdah" (روضة) means a place that has abundant water and trees; hence, lush and green gardens are called "Rawdah." If we see that this word appears here in the indefinite form (Nakirah), it is for the purpose of glorification and magnification—meaning they will be immersed in blessings in the best and most superior of the beautiful and joy-inducing gardens of Paradise.

"But as for those who disbelieved and denied Our signs and the meeting of the Hereafter, those will be brought forth for the divine punishment" (وَ أَمَّا الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا وَ لِقاءِ الْآخِرَةِ فَأُولئِكَ فِي الْعَذابِ مُحْضَرُونَ). It is interesting that regarding the people of Paradise, the expression "Yuhbarun" is used, which indicates their comprehensive satisfaction. However, regarding the people of Hell, He uses the term "Muhdarun" (brought forth/summoned), which is evidence of their extreme reluctance and displeasure, because "summoning" (Ihdar) is applied in cases where it occurs against a person's inner will.

Another point: regarding the people of Paradise, He stipulates both "faith" and "righteous deeds," whereas regarding the people of Hell, He contents Himself only with the lack of faith (denial of the Origin and the Resurrection). This is an indication that for entry into Paradise, faith alone is not enough—righteous deeds are also necessary. However, for entry into Hell, the lack of faith is sufficient, even if no other sin had been committed by him, for disbelief itself is the greatest sin!

Key Points Why one of the names of the Resurrection is "The Hour" (al-Sa'ah)

It is also necessary to pay attention to the fact that in many verses of the Quran, including two of the verses under discussion, the occurrence of the Resurrection is referred to as the "Establishment of the Hour" (Qiyam al-Sa'ah). This is because "Sa'ah" (ساعة) originally means a portion of time or fleeting moments. Since, on one hand, the occurrence of the Resurrection is sudden and lightning-fast, and on the other hand, according to Allah being "Swift in Reckoning," He will settle the accounts of the servants on that day with speed, this expression is used regarding the Resurrection so that people always keep the situation of the Resurrection in mind.

Ibn Manzur says in Lisan al-Arab: "Sa'ah" is a name for the time when the Shout of the end of the world is sounded and everyone suddenly dies, and it is also a name for the time when people are resurrected on the Day of Judgment. This name was chosen for the end of the world and the occurrence of the Resurrection because, at the first Shout—which Allah referred to in the verse: "It was but a single Shout, and behold! they were silent and still" [1]—everyone suddenly dies (and at the second blast, everyone suddenly rises and the Resurrection is established).

Zabidi in Taj al-Arus quotes from some that "Sa'ah" is of three types:

  1. "The Great Hour" (al-Sa'at al-Kubra): The Day of Resurrection and the reviving of people for the Reckoning.
  2. "The Middle Hour" (al-Sa'at al-Wusta): The day of the sudden death of the people of a specific time (by divine punishments and annihilating torments).
  3. "The Lesser Hour" (al-Sa'at al-Sughra): The day of the death of every individual human being.

[1]: Surah Ya-Sin, Verse 29.

Ayahs

So glorify Allah when you enter evening and when you rise at dawn. (17)
وَلَهُ ٱلْحَمْدُ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَعَشِيًّا وَحِينَ تُظْهِرُونَ (١٨)
To Him belongs all praise in the heavens and the earth, at nightfall and when you enter noontime. (18)
يُخْرِجُ ٱلْحَىَّ مِنَ ٱلْمَيِّتِ وَيُخْرِجُ ٱلْمَيِّتَ مِنَ ٱلْحَىِّ وَيُحْىِ ٱلْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَا ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ تُخْرَجُونَ (١٩)
