Commentary

Glorification and Praise Belong to Allah at All Times

After the extensive discussions in the preceding verses regarding the Origin and the Resurrection, and parts of the reward for the believers and the punishment for the polytheists, the verses under discussion turn to the glorification and praise of the Lord, and His sanctification and purification from any kind of shirk (polytheism), deficiency, or defect, stating: "So glory be to Allah when you enter the evening and when you enter the morning" (فَسُبْحانَ اللَّهِ حِينَ تُمْسُونَ وَ حِينَ تُصْبِحُونَ‌).

"And to Him belongs [all] praise throughout the heavens and the earth. And [glorify Him] in the afternoon and when you are at noon" (وَ لَهُ الْحَمْدُ فِي السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ عَشِيًّا وَ حِينَ تُظْهِرُونَ‌).

Thus, in these two verses, four times are mentioned for the "glorification" (tasbih) of the Lord:

  1. The beginning of the night (حِينَ تُمْسُونَ‌).
  2. The break of dawn (وَ حِينَ تُصْبِحُونَ‌).
  3. The afternoon (وَ عَشِيًّا).
  4. And at the time of the sun's decline at noon (وَ حِينَ تُظْهِرُونَ‌) [1]. However, "praise" (hamd) has been generalized in terms of location, encompassing the expanse of the heavens and the earth.

The mention of these four times in the above verses may be an allusion to the continuity and permanence of glorification, just as we say in Persian, "Watch over so-and-so every morning and evening" (meaning always and at all times).

Another possibility suggested by some commentators is that the four aforementioned times refer to the times of the prayers. However, they have not answered the question of why only four times are mentioned instead of five (and why the time of the Isha prayer is not mentioned).

Yet, it could be answered that since the times for Maghrib and Isha prayers are relatively close to each other—the interval between them being about one to one and a half hours—they are mentioned together, whereas the interval for the preferred time (fadilah) of Dhuhr and Asr is relatively long, spanning several hours.

However, if we take glorification and praise in their broad sense in the above verses, they will not be limited

[1]: Note that "عَشِيًّا" (in the afternoon) and "حِينَ تُظْهِرُونَ‌" (when you are at noon) are coordinated with "حين تمسون" (when you enter the evening) and refer back to the subject of glorification.

to the five daily prayers, even though these prayers are among their clearest manifestations.

It is also necessary to note that the phrases "Subhan Allah" (Glory be to Allah) and "wa lahu al-hamd" (and to Him belongs praise) could be an initiation (insha') of glorification and praise by Allah Himself, just as He says in Surah Al-Mu'minun, verse 14: "فَتَبارَكَ اللَّهُ أَحْسَنُ الْخالِقِينَ‌" ("So blessed is Allah, the best of creators").

It is also possible that this praise and glorification are in the sense of a command, meaning: "Glorify Him and praise Him" (subbihuhu wa-ahmadu lahu). This interpretation seems closer—that the above verses are an instruction to all servants to praise and glorify Allah every morning and evening, and every noon and afternoon, through prayer and otherwise, in order to cleanse the effects of shirk and sin from their hearts and souls.

In a hadith from the noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated: "Whoever recites the two verses above and the verse following them in the morning will compensate for what he misses during the day, and whoever recites them at the beginning of the night will compensate for what he misses during the night." [1]

In the following verse, the text returns to the issue of the Resurrection, answering the skepticism of the deniers through another path: "He brings the living out of the dead and brings the dead out of the living and brings to life the earth after its lifelessness. And thus will you be brought out" (يُخْرِجُ الْحَيَّ مِنَ الْمَيِّتِ وَ يُخْرِجُ الْمَيِّتَ مِنَ الْحَيِّ وَ يُحْيِ الْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِها وَ كَذلِكَ تُخْرَجُونَ‌).

This means that the "scene of the Resurrection" and the "scene of the end of the world"—one being the emergence of "the living from the dead" and the other "the dead from the living"—are constantly repeated before your eyes. Therefore,

[1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 172.

what cause is there for wonder that at the end of the world all living beings should die, and at the Resurrection all humans should return to a new life?! As for "bringing the living out of the dead" regarding dead lands—which the Quran repeatedly emphasizes for the issue of Resurrection—it is clear to everyone that in the winter season, the earth becomes dead; no plant grows from it, no flower smiles, and no blossom blooms. But in the spring season, with the moderation of the weather and the falling of life-giving drops of rain, a movement arises in the earth; plants grow everywhere, flowers smile, and blossoms appear on the branches. This is the scene of the Resurrection that we witness in this world.

As for bringing the dead out of the living, that too is not something hidden; constantly on the surface of the earth, trees die and turn into dry wood, and humans and animals lose their lives and turn into lifeless corpses.

Regarding "the emergence of a living being from the dead," some commentators have interpreted it as the emergence of humans and animals from a drop of semen (nutfah), some as the birth of a believer from a disbeliever, and some as the awakening of those who sleep.

However, it appears that the primary meaning of the verse is none of these, because the nutfah itself is a living entity, and the issue of faith and disbelief belongs to the inner depths (batn) of the verse, not its literal surface (zahir). The subject of sleep and wakefulness is metaphorical, as they are not actual life and death.

The literal meaning of the verse is that Allah constantly brings living beings out of dead matter and transforms lifeless substances into living ones.

Although, according to the certainties of modern science, at least in human experiments and daily observations, no case has been seen where living beings are born from lifeless matter; rather, living beings are always born from the egg, seed, or nutfah of another living being. However, certainly in the beginning, when this terrestrial globe was a mass of fire, no living being existed. Later, under specific conditions that science has not yet fully discovered, living beings were born from lifeless materials through a "great leap." This phenomenon is not seen under the current conditions of the earth where it is accessible to human knowledge (though perhaps in the depths of the oceans, under certain conditions, this great leap still occurs).

But what is perceptible and completely tangible to us is that dead matter constantly becomes part of the organs of living beings and dons the garment of life. The water and food we consume are not living beings, but once they become part of our body, they transform into a living entity, and new cells are added to the cells of our body, just as a nursing infant becomes a powerful, large-bodied youth through this very path.

Is this not bringing life out of the heart of death, and "the living" out of "the dead"? Therefore, it can be said: in the system of the natural world, life constantly emerges from the heart of death and death from the heart of life. For this reason, the God who is the Creator of nature is capable of reviving the dead in the other world.

Of course—as we mentioned—the above verse also has other interpretations from a spiritual dimension, including the birth of a believer from a disbeliever, a disbeliever from a believer, a scholar from an ignorant person, an ignorant person from a scholar, a righteous person from a corrupt one, and a corrupt person from a righteous one, as has been pointed out in some Islamic narrations.

These meanings may be from the inner depths (batn) of the verse, as we know that the verses of the Quran have an outer and an inner meaning. It is also possible that "death" and "life" have a comprehensive and broad meaning that includes both material and spiritual aspects.

In a narration from Imam Musa ibn Ja'far عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of the verse "He brings to life the earth after its lifelessness," it is stated:

ليس يحييها بالقطر و لكن يبعث اللَّه رجالا فيحيون العدل فتحى الارض لاحياء العدل، و لاقامة العدل فيه انفع فى الارض من القطر اربعين صباحا:

"It does not mean that He revives it with rain; rather, Allah raises men who revive the principles of justice, and the earth is revived through the revival of justice. (Know that) the establishment of justice on earth is more beneficial for the earth than forty days of rain." [1] It is clear that when the Imam عليه السلام says "it does not mean the falling of rain," he is denying exclusivity; meaning the verse should not be interpreted exclusively as rain, because the spiritual revival of the earth through justice is even more important than the falling of rain.

[1]: Quoted from the book "Al-Kafi" as cited in "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 4, p. 173.

Ayahs

وَمِنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦٓ أَنْ خَلَقَكُم مِّن تُرَابٍ ثُمَّ إِذَآ أَنتُم بَشَرٌ تَنتَشِرُونَ (٢٠)
Of His signs is that He created you from dust, then, behold, you are humans scattering [all over]! (20)
وَمِنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦٓ أَنْ خَلَقَ لَكُم مِّنْ أَنفُسِكُمْ أَزْوَٰجًا لِّتَسْكُنُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهَا وَجَعَلَ بَيْنَكُم مَّوَدَّةً وَرَحْمَةً ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ (٢١)
And of His signs is that He created for you mates from your own selves that you may take comfort in them, and He ordained affection and mercy between you. There are indeed signs in that for a people who reflect. (21)
وَمِنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ خَلْقُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱخْتِلَـٰفُ أَلْسِنَتِكُمْ وَأَلْوَٰنِكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّلْعَـٰلِمِينَ (٢٢)
Among His signs is the creation of the heavens and the earth, and the difference of your languages and colours. There are indeed signs in that for those who know. (22)

Commentary

Signs of Allah in the Horizons and the Souls!

These verses, along with a portion of the subsequent verses, recount fascinating points regarding the proofs of Monotheism (Tawhid) and the signs of the Lord within the system of the universe, thereby completing the previous discussions.

It can be said that, taken together, these verses constitute a significant portion of the Quranic verses related to Monotheism.

These verses—all of which begin with the expression min ayatih (Among His signs...) and possess a specific rhythm, a captivating and attractive tone, and effective and profound expressions—consist of seven verses in total. Six of them appear consecutively, while one verse is separate (Verse 46 of this same Surah).

These seven verses feature an interesting classification in terms of "Horizonal" (afaqi) and "Self-bound" (anfusi) signs. Specifically, three verses discuss the anfusi signs (the signs of Allah within the human being's own existence), three verses discuss the afaqi signs (the signs of the Lord's greatness outside the human being), and one verse speaks of both anfusi and afaqi signs.

It is noteworthy that there are only eleven verses in the Quran that begin with this phrase, seven of which are in this Surah (Ar-Rum), two in Surah Fussilat (Verses 37 and 39), and two in Surah Ash-Shura (Verses 29 and 32). Collectively, these eleven verses truly represent a complete course in Monotheism.

Before entering the commentary of the verses, we deem it necessary to mention that although the matters the Quran refers to in these verses are understandable and recognizable to the general public at first glance, with the advancement of human science and knowledge, new points regarding them are constantly revealed to scientists. We will refer to some of these in the course of the commentary on these verses.

Here, the Quran first turns to the creation of man, which is the first and most important divine blessing upon him, and says: "And among His signs is that He created you from dust; then, suddenly you were human beings dispersing [throughout the earth]" (وَ مِنْ آياتِهِ أَنْ خَلَقَكُمْ مِنْ تُرابٍ ثُمَّ إِذا أَنْتُمْ بَشَرٌ تَنْتَشِرُونَ‌).

In this verse, two signs of Divine greatness are mentioned: first, the creation of man from dust, which may refer to the creation of the first human, namely Adam, or the creation of all humans from dust, since the nutritional substances that form the human existence are all—directly or indirectly—derived from the earth.

The second is the multiplication of the human race and the dispersal of the children of Adam across the entire earth. Had the characteristic of expansion not been created within man, he would have soon perished and his lineage would have been cut off.

Truly, how vast is the distance between dust and a human being of such delicacy?

If we were to place the delicate layers of the eye—which are softer, more sensitive, and finer than a flower petal—as well as the extraordinarily sensitive and delicate cells of the brain next to dust and compare them, we would then realize what marvelous power the Creator of the universe has employed to bring forth such delicate, precise, and precious systems from that dark, low-value material.

Dust has neither light, nor heat, nor beauty, nor freshness, nor sensation, nor movement; yet, it has become the raw material for a human being possessing all these attributes. He who creates such a wondrous living being from such a dead entity—which is considered among the least valuable of existences—is worthy of every praise for this immeasurable power and knowledge: "So blessed be Allah, the Best of creators" (فَتَبارَكَ اللَّهُ أَحْسَنُ الْخالِقِينَ‌).

This expression also conveys the reality that there is no difference among human beings and that the root of all leads back to one place; all have an unbreakable bond with the earth, and naturally, in the end, all shall return to that same earth.

It is noteworthy that the word idha in the Arabic language is usually used for sudden occurrences. The mention of this expression here may indicate that Allah gave Adam such power of reproduction that, in a short period, his descendants suddenly dispersed across the earth and formed an organized human society.

The second verse under discussion addresses another part of the anfusi signs, which follows the stage of human creation, stating: "And among His signs is that He created for you mates from among yourselves, that you may find tranquility in them" (وَ مِنْ آياتِهِ أَنْ خَلَقَ لَكُمْ مِنْ أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَزْواجاً لِتَسْكُنُوا إِلَيْها). And since the continuation of this bond between spouses specifically, and among all humans generally, requires a heart-felt and spiritual attraction, He adds: "and He placed between you affection and mercy" (وَ جَعَلَ بَيْنَكُمْ مَوَدَّةً وَ رَحْمَةً).

At the end of the verse, for further emphasis, He says: "Indeed in that are signs for a people who give thought" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ‌).

It is interesting that in this verse, the Quran identifies the goal of marriage as "dwelling" and "tranquility," and through the meaningful expression li-taskunu (that you may find tranquility), it conveys many matters. A similar expression also appears in Verse 189 of Surah Al-A'raf.

Truly, the existence of spouses with these characteristics for humans, which serves as the source of peace in their lives, is considered one of the great Divine blessings.

This tranquility stems from the fact that these two genders complement one another and are the cause of each other's flourishing, joy, and growth, such that each is incomplete without the other. It is natural that such a powerful attraction exists between a being and its complement.

From this, one can conclude that those who turn their backs on this Divine tradition possess an incomplete existence, for one stage of their perfection has been halted (unless specific circumstances and necessity truly mandate celibacy).

In any case, this tranquility and dwelling are both physical and spiritual, individual and social.

The physical illnesses that arise for the human body due to the abandonment of marriage cannot be denied. Similarly, the spiritual imbalance and psychological restlessness that celibate individuals struggle with are more or less clear to everyone.

From a social perspective, celibate individuals feel less responsibility, and for this reason, suicide is seen more frequently among the unmarried, and horrific crimes are also more often committed by them.

When a person steps from the stage of celibacy into the stage of family life, they find a new personality within themselves and feel a greater sense of responsibility; this is the meaning of feeling tranquility under the shadow of marriage.

As for the issue of "affection" (mawaddah) and "mercy" (rahmah), they are in fact the "mortar" and "glue" for the building materials of human society; for society is composed of individual human beings, much like a vast and magnificent building composed of bricks and pieces of stone.

If these scattered individuals and those various parts do not find a connection and bond with one another, no "society" or "building" will come into existence.

He who created man for social life has also placed this necessary bond and connection within his soul.

The difference between "affection" (mawaddah) and "mercy" (rahmah) may be from several perspectives:

  1. "Affection" is the motive for connection at the beginning, but in the end, when one of the two spouses may become weak and frail and unable to provide service, "mercy" takes its place.

  2. "Affection" pertains to adults who can serve one another, but children and young offspring are nurtured under the shadow of "mercy."

  3. "Affection" is often mutual, but mercy is one-sided and self-sacrificing; for the survival of a society, sometimes mutual services are necessary—the source of which is affection—and sometimes unconditional services are needed, which require sacrifice and "mercy."

Of course, the verse describes affection and mercy between two spouses, but the possibility also exists that the expression baynakum (between you) refers to all human beings, with the two spouses being one of its most prominent examples. This is because not only family life, but life in the entire human society is impossible without these two principles—namely "affection" and "mercy"—and the disappearance of these two bonds, or even their weakness and deficiency, is the cause of thousands of miseries, hardships, and social anxieties.

The last verse under discussion is a blend of afaqi and anfusi signs: first, it refers to the issue of the creation of the heavens and the earth, saying: "And among His signs is the creation of the heavens and the earth" (وَ مِنْ آياتِهِ خَلْقُ‌ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ). The heavens with all those celestial bodies, with all those solar systems and galaxies—heavens whose grandeur the high-flying intellect of man is incapable of perceiving, and the mind becomes exhausted from studying them. The more human science and knowledge advance, the more new points of their greatness are revealed.

There was a day when man considered the stars of the sky to be only the number seen with the eye (scientists have counted those visible to the naked eye as approximately five to six thousand).

However, as more powerful and massive telescopes were built, the greatness and multitude of the stars in the sky increased, to the point that today they believe our galaxy alone—which is just one of the many galaxies in the sky—has more than one hundred million stars, of which our sun, with its dazzling greatness, is considered one of the average stars! And only Allah knows how many stars exist in all the galaxies, the number of which is clear to no one.

Similarly, as the natural sciences, geology, botany, zoology, anatomy, physiology, psychology, and psychoanalysis advance, new wonders regarding the creation of the earth are discovered, each of which is a sign among the signs of Allah's greatness.

Then, the discourse shifts to one of the great anfusi signs, saying: "and the diversity of your languages and your colors" (وَ اخْتِلافُ أَلْسِنَتِكُمْ وَ أَلْوانِكُمْ‌).

Undoubtedly, the social life of mankind is impossible without "recognizing individuals and persons." If one day all humans were of one shape, one appearance, and one stature, the fabric of their lives would unravel in that very day. Neither father, child, nor spouse would be distinguished from a stranger; nor the criminal from the innocent, the debtor from the creditor, the commander from the subordinate, the leader from the led, the host from the guest, or the friend from the foe. What a strange chaos would ensue! Occasionally, this issue arises regarding twin brothers who resemble each other in every respect, and what difficulties occur in people's encounters and relations with them—to the point that we have heard that sometimes one of the identical twin brothers was ill, and the mother gave the medicine to the other! Therefore, to organize human society, Allah has made voices and colors diverse.

According to Fakhr al-Razi in his commentary on the verse under discussion, human recognition of another human must be achieved either through the "eye" or by means of the "ear." For the recognition of the eye, Allah created colors, faces, and shapes differently; and for the recognition of the ear, He created the diversity of voices and vocal tones, such that in the entire world, one cannot find two humans who are identical in every respect regarding their face and vocal tone. That is, the human face—which is a small organ—and the human vocal tone—which is a simple matter—take on billions of different forms by the power of the Lord, and this is among the signs of His greatness.

Of course, the possibility also exists—and some great commentators have pointed to it—that "diversity of languages" (ikhtilaf al-alsinah) refers to the difference in languages such as Arabic, Persian, and the like, and "diversity of colors" refers to the difference in races, where each race has a color.

However, a broad meaning can be derived from the word "diversity" (ikhtilaf) that includes both this interpretation and the previous one. In any sense, this variety of creation is a witness to His greatness and power.

Farid Wajdi, in his Encyclopedia, quotes the famous Western scientist Newton as saying: "Never doubt the Creator of the universe and God, for it is not rational that necessity and unconscious cause and effect alone should be the leader of existence. For a blind and uniform necessity in every place and every time cannot be imagined to produce all these diverse creations and colorful beings. It is impossible that existence should appear with the order and arrangement of its parts and the necessary proportions, harmonious with the changes of time and place; rather, all these matters must certainly originate from a Source that possesses knowledge, wisdom, and will." [1]

At the end of the aforementioned verse, the Quran says: "Indeed in that are signs for those of knowledge" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِلْعالِمِينَ‌).

For they, more than anyone else, become aware of these secrets.

[1]: Farid Wajdi Encyclopedia, Volume 1, Page 496 (Entry: Ilah).

Ayahs

وَمِنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ مَنَامُكُم بِٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ وَٱبْتِغَآؤُكُم مِّن فَضْلِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَسْمَعُونَ (٢٣)
And of His signs is your sleep by night and day, and your pursuit of His bounty. There are indeed signs in that for a people who listen. (23)
وَمِنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ يُرِيكُمُ ٱلْبَرْقَ خَوْفًا وَطَمَعًا وَيُنَزِّلُ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَيُحْىِۦ بِهِ ٱلْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَآ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ (٢٤)
And of His signs is that He shows you the lightning, arousing fear and hope, and He sends down water from the sky, and with it revives the earth after its death. There are indeed signs in that for people who exercise their reason. (24)
وَمِنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦٓ أَن تَقُومَ ٱلسَّمَآءُ وَٱلْأَرْضُ بِأَمْرِهِۦ ۚ ثُمَّ إِذَا دَعَاكُمْ دَعْوَةً مِّنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ إِذَآ أَنتُمْ تَخْرُجُونَ (٢٥)
And of His signs is that the sky and the earth stand by His command, and then, when He calls you forth from the earth, behold, you will come forth. (25)

Commentary

Once Again, Signs of His Greatness in the Outer and Inner Worlds

Following the previous discussions regarding the signs of the Lord in the horizons and within the souls, the verses under discussion address another part of these great signs.

First, the phenomenon of "sleep" is highlighted as an important phenomenon of creation and an illustration of the wise system of its Creator, stating:

"And of His signs is your sleep by night and day, and your seeking of His bounty and providing for the requirements of life" (وَ مِنْ آياتِهِ مَنامُكُمْ بِاللَّيْلِ وَ النَّهارِ وَ ابْتِغاؤُكُمْ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ).

And at the end, it adds: "Indeed in that are signs for a people who listen" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِقَوْمٍ يَسْمَعُونَ).

This reality is hidden from no one: that all "living beings" need rest to renew their strength and gain the necessary readiness to continue work and activity—a rest that comes to them by necessity, forcing even the most hardworking or greedy individuals to undertake it.

What factor can be imagined better for achieving this goal than sleep, which necessarily comes to man and compels him to suspend all physical activities and a significant portion of his intellectual and cerebral activities? Only those systems of the body, such as the heart and lungs, and parts of brain activity necessary for the continuation of life, continue their work, and even then, very calmly and slowly.

This great divine blessing causes the human body and soul to be, so to speak, "serviced." With the onset of the state of sleep, which is a type of pause and cessation of the body's work, tranquility and the relief of fatigue are achieved, and man finds new life, vitality, and strength.

Certainly, if there were no sleep, the human soul and body would wither and wear out very quickly, and old age and frailty would come upon him very soon. For this reason, appropriate and peaceful sleep is the secret of health, longevity, and the endurance of youthful vitality.

It is noteworthy that, "firstly," sleep is mentioned before "seeking the bounty of Allah" (which in Quranic verses means striving for sustenance), indicating that it is considered a foundation for it; for without sufficient sleep, "seeking the bounty of Allah" is difficult.

"Secondly," while it is true that sleep is usually at night and the struggle for livelihood is during the day, it is not such that man cannot change this schedule when necessary. Rather, Allah has created man such that he can change his sleep schedule and adapt it to necessities and needs. The expression "your sleep by night and day" (مَنامُكُمْ بِاللَّيْلِ وَ النَّهارِ) seemingly points to this very point.

Undoubtedly, the primary schedule for sleep pertains to the night, and the night has a special priority in this regard because of the tranquility arising from darkness. However, sometimes conditions arise in human life where, for example, one is forced to travel by night and rest by day. If the mechanism for regulating sleep were not at man's disposal, what difficulties would arise?

The importance of this matter is clearer than ever, especially in our era, where many industrial, medical, and healthcare institutions are forced to work continuously throughout the day and night, and suspending their programs is impossible; thus, workers operate in three shifts.

The need of the human body and soul for sleep is so great that man's ability and endurance against sleeplessness are very low and do not exceed a few days and nights. For this reason, the prevention of sleep has always been known as one of the most painful tortures among tyrants and oppressors.

Also for this reason, one of the effective methods for treating many diseases is to put the patient into a deep sleep, thereby increasing the patient's power and strength.

Of course, no one can determine a specific amount as the "required amount of sleep" for all humans, as this matter depends on age, the condition and situation of individuals, and the nature of their spiritual and physical makeup. What is important is that "sufficient sleep" is the amount after which a person feels saturated in this regard, just as one feels full regarding water and the amount of food.

It is also noteworthy that in addition to the "length" of sleep time, its "depth" is also of special importance. Often, one hour of deep sleep performs the work of several hours of superficial sleep in reconstructing the human soul and body.

Of course, where deep sleep is not possible, "nu'as" (light sleep) is also one of the divine blessings, as mentioned in verse 11 of Surah Al-Anfal regarding the warriors of Badr; for on the battlefield, deep sleep is neither possible nor beneficial.

In any case, the blessing of sleep, the tranquility and comfort resulting from it, and the power and vitality found after sleep are among the blessings that cannot be described by any expression.

The next verse, which states the fifth part of the signs of God's greatness, returns to the signs in the horizons, drawing attention to the issue of rain, lightning, and the life of the earth after death, saying: "And of His signs is that He shows you the lightning, [causing] fear and hope" (وَ مِنْ آياتِهِ يُرِيكُمُ الْبَرْقَ خَوْفاً وَ طَمَعاً).

"Fear" of the dangers arising from lightning, which sometimes takes the form of a "thunderbolt" (sa'iqah) and burns and turns to ash anything within its range; and "hope" regarding the descent of rain, which often pours down as a shower after thunder and lightning.

Therefore, the lightning of the sky is a precursor to the descent of rain (in addition to the various important benefits hidden in this lightning, which modern science has unveiled and which we mentioned at the beginning of Surah Ar-Ra'd) [1]. Then it adds: "and He sends down water from the sky, giving life thereby to the earth after its death" (وَ يُنَزِّلُ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً فَيُحْيِي بِهِ الْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِها).

The dry and scorching earth, from which the scent of death emanates everywhere, takes on such life and becomes so revived after the descent of a few life-giving rains—and the signs of life appear in it in the form of flowers and plants—that it is sometimes unbelievable that this is the same formerly dead earth.

At the end of the verse, as an emphasis, it adds: "Indeed in that are signs for a people who use reason" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ).

It is they who understand that in this calculated program, a hand of power is at work, leading and guiding it, and it can never be the effect of blind and deaf accidents or necessities.

[1]: Refer to Volume 10 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 149 onwards.

In the final verse, the discussion of the signs in the horizons continues regarding the management of the system of the heavens and the earth and their stability and permanence, stating: "And of His signs is that the heaven and earth stand by His command" (وَ مِنْ آياتِهِ أَنْ تَقُومَ السَّماءُ وَ الْأَرْضُ بِأَمْرِهِ).

That is, not only is the creation of the heavens—which was mentioned in the previous verses—a sign, but the standing and continuation of their system is also another sign; for these massive celestial bodies, in their regular rotation, need many things, the most important of which is the complex calculation of the balance between the forces of gravity and repulsion.

The Great Lord has organized this balance such that they rotate in their paths for millions of years without the slightest deviation.

In other words, the previous verse referred to "Monotheism in Creation" (Tawhid al-Khaliqiyyah), and this verse refers to "Monotheism in Lordship and Governance" (Tawhid al-Rububiyyah).

The expression "standing" (qiyam) of the heaven and earth is a subtle metaphor taken from human states, as the best state for a human to continue activities is the state of standing, in which he is capable of fulfilling all his needs and has full control over his surroundings.

The expression "command" (amr) here refers to the ultimate power of the Lord, such that for the continuation of life and the order of this vast world, only one command of His is sufficient.

At the end of this verse, using "Monotheism" as a background for "the Resurrection," the discussion shifts to this issue, stating: "Then when He calls you with a [single] call from the earth, immediately you will come forth" (ثُمَّ إِذا دَعاكُمْ دَعْوَةً مِنَ الْأَرْضِ إِذا أَنْتُمْ تَخْرُجُونَ).

We have repeatedly seen in the verses of the Quran that Allah proves the issue of the Resurrection by relying on the signs of His power in the heavens and the earth, and the verse under discussion is one of them.

The expression "He calls you" (دعاكم) indicates that just as one command of His is sufficient for the governance and order of the world, one call of His suffices for the raising, gathering, and resurrection. Especially considering the phrase "immediately you will come forth" (إِذا أَنْتُمْ تَخْرُجُونَ), where "idha" is for "suddenness" (mufaja'ah), it clarifies that with one call of His, everyone will suddenly pour out.

Furthermore, the expression "a call from the earth" (دَعْوَةً مِنَ الْأَرْضِ) is a clear sign of physical resurrection—that man is called from this earth on the Day of Resurrection (reflect on this).

Key Points

1- A Complete Course in Theology

In the past six verses, various discussions regarding theology have been raised, which together form an interesting course: from the creation of the heavens to the creation of man from dust, and from the bond of family love to restful sleep in the night and day, from the governance of the system and the world above to the lightning of the sky, the descent of rain, and the diversity of languages and colors—that is, a suitable collection of signs in the horizons and within the souls.

Interestingly, in each of these six verses, two parts of the evidences for Monotheism are mentioned, so that one prepares the ground and the other reinforces and emphasizes, just like bringing two just witnesses to prove a claim, forming a total of twelve truthful witnesses for the infinite power of the Truth!

2- Who Gains Inspiration from These Signs?

At the end of four of these six verses, it is emphasized that in these matters are clear signs for "those who reflect," "those who know," "those who listen," and "those who use reason," but this is not seen in the first and last verses.

Fakhr al-Razi explains this as follows: The absence of its mention in the first verse may be because the first and second verses are consecutive and both speak of signs within the soul and are similar.

In the last verse, the matter has become so clear that there is no longer a need for further explanation or emphasis on reasoning and reflection [1]. Interestingly, it first speaks of "reflection" (tafakkur), then of "knowledge" ('ilm), because reflection is the foundation and groundwork for knowledge. After that, it speaks of "a listening ear," because in the light of knowledge and awareness, man becomes ready to hear and accept the truth, just as the Quran says: "So give good tidings to My servants who listen to speech and follow the best of it" (Surah Az-Zumar, verse 18).

In the final stage, it speaks of "reason" ('aql), because those who have a listening ear will eventually reach the stage of complete reason.

It is also noteworthy that at the end of the first verse, the talk is of the beginning of human creation and the spreading of his progeny on earth (ثُمَّ إِذا أَنْتُمْ بَشَرٌ تَنْتَشِرُونَ).

And in the last verse, the talk is of the coming forth and resurrection of humans on the Day of Judgment (إِذا أَنْتُمْ تَخْرُجُونَ).

The first is the beginning, and the last is the end.

3- The Wonders of the World of Sleep

Despite all the discussions scientists have had regarding sleep and its characteristics, it still seems that not all angles of this mysterious world have been clarified, and its complex realities have not been fully revealed.

There is still debate among scientists as to what reactions take place in the human body such that in a sudden moment, a part of the activities of his brain and body are suspended, and a transformation appears throughout his soul and body.

Some consider the main factor of sleep to be a "physical factor" and believe that the transfer of blood from the brain to other parts of the body creates this phenomenon. To prove their opinion, they have used a special bed called a "scale bed" that identifies the transfer of blood from the brain to other organs.

Another group considers the factor of sleep to be a "chemical factor" and believes that during exertion and effort, toxins are produced in the body that disable part of the brain, resulting in the person falling asleep; when these toxins are absorbed by the body and neutralized, the person wakes up.

Another group posits a "nervous factor" for sleep and says there is a special active nervous system in the brain that acts like the accelerator of a car and shuts down due to fatigue, temporarily ceasing to function.

However, regarding all these theories, there are questions and vague, dark points that have not yet been clearly answered, and sleep still maintains its mysterious face.

Among the wonders of the world of sleep that scientists have recently uncovered is that during sleep and the temporary shutdown of a large part of the brain, some cells—which should be called "guard cells"—remain awake and never forget the instructions man gives them before sleep regarding the moment of waking up, until they wake the entire brain and set it in motion when necessary.

For example, a weary and exhausted mother who sleeps at night while her nursing infant is in the cradle near her, unconsciously instructs the guard cell—which is the link between soul and body—that "whenever my child makes the slightest sound, wake me up, but other noises are not important!" Therefore, it is possible that a clap of thunder does not wake this mother from sleep, but the slightest sound from her child causes her to wake. This important duty has been undertaken by that very guard cell! We ourselves have also experienced this many times: whenever we intend to go on a trip or an important program early in the morning or even in the middle of the night and entrust this to ourselves, we often wake up on time, whereas at other times we might remain in sleep for hours.

In short, since sleep is among the spiritual phenomena, and the soul is a world full of secrets, it is not surprising that the angles of this issue have not yet been clarified. But the more we delve into and examine it, the more we become acquainted with the greatness of the Creator of this phenomenon.

All of this was regarding sleep; as for dreams (ru'ya) and dreaming, there are many discussions which we have provided in the commentary of Surah Yusuf, Volume 9, page 312 onwards.

4- The Bond of Love Between Two Spouses

Although the connection of a human with his parents and brother is a "lineal" connection and stems from deep roots of kinship, and the bond of two spouses is a contractual and legal bond, it often happens that the love and affection arising from it even surpasses the kinship affection for parents. This is, in fact, what is referred to in the above verses with the phrase: "and He placed between you affection and mercy" (وَ جَعَلَ بَيْنَكُمْ مَوَدَّةً وَ رَحْمَةً).

In a hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that after the Battle of Uhud:

He said to the "daughter of Jahsh": "Your maternal uncle Hamza was martyred." She said: "Indeed we belong to Allah and indeed to Him we will return (إِنَّا لِلَّهِ وَ إِنَّا إِلَيْهِ راجِعُونَ); I seek the reward for this calamity from Allah."

He said to her again: "Your brother was also martyred." Once more she said "Inna lillah" and sought her reward from Allah.

But as soon as he gave her the news of her husband's martyrdom, she put her hand on her head and cried out. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: (Yes) "Nothing equals the husband in the view of a woman" [1].

[1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Volume 4, page 174.

Ayahs

وَلَهُۥ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ كُلٌّ لَّهُۥ قَـٰنِتُونَ (٢٦)
To Him belongs whoever is in the heavens and the earth. All are obedient to Him. (26)
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى يَبْدَؤُا۟ ٱلْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُۥ وَهُوَ أَهْوَنُ عَلَيْهِ ۚ وَلَهُ ٱلْمَثَلُ ٱلْأَعْلَىٰ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٢٧)
It is He who originates the creation, and then He will bring it back—and that is more simple for Him. His is the loftiest description in the heavens and the earth, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise. (27)
ضَرَبَ لَكُم مَّثَلًا مِّنْ أَنفُسِكُمْ ۖ هَل لَّكُم مِّن مَّا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُكُم مِّن شُرَكَآءَ فِى مَا رَزَقْنَـٰكُمْ فَأَنتُمْ فِيهِ سَوَآءٌ تَخَافُونَهُمْ كَخِيفَتِكُمْ أَنفُسَكُمْ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ نُفَصِّلُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ (٢٨)
He draws for you an example from yourselves: Do you have among your slaves any partners [who may share] in what We have provided you, so that you are equal in its respect, and you revere them as you revere one another? Thus do We elaborate the signs for people who exercise their reason. (28)
بَلِ ٱتَّبَعَ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوٓا۟ أَهْوَآءَهُم بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ ۖ فَمَن يَهْدِى مَنْ أَضَلَّ ٱللَّهُ ۖ وَمَا لَهُم مِّن نَّـٰصِرِينَ (٢٩)
Indeed, the wrongdoers follow their own desires without any knowledge. So who will guide those whom Allah has led astray? They will have no helpers. (29)

Commentary

The Oneness of God’s Ownership

In the preceding verses, discussions regarding the "Oneness of Creation" (Tawhid al-Khaliqiyyah) and the "Oneness of Lordship" (Tawhid al-Rububiyyah) were presented. The first verse under discussion speaks of another branch of monotheism, which is the "Oneness of Ownership" (Tawhid al-Malikiyyah).

It states: "To Him belongs whoever is in the heavens and the earth" (وَلَهُ مَنْ فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ).

And since all belong to Him, "all are devoutly obedient to Him" (كُلٌّ لَهُ قَانِتُونَ).

It is clear that the intended meaning of "ownership" as well as "obedience" here is generative (takwini) ownership and obedience. This means that according to the laws of creation, the reins of all affairs are in His hand, and everyone—willingly or unwillingly—is submitted to His laws in the primordial world of existence.

Even the rebellious tyrants and law-breaking sinners are compelled to bow their heads before God’s generative laws.

The proof for this "Ownership" is His very "Creation" and "Lordship": He who created beings in the beginning and took charge of their management must certainly be the true Owner, and none other.

Since all beings in the universe are equal in this regard, it becomes clear that He has no partner in ownership. Even the imaginary deities of the polytheists are themselves owned by the King of Kings and are subject to His command.

Furthermore, it should be noted that qanit is derived from the root qunut, which originally means the persistence of obedience coupled with humility (as Raghib stated in Al-Mufradat).

In a hadith from the noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "Every qunut in the Quran means obedience."

However, sometimes this obedience is generative (takwini) and sometimes it is legislative (tashri'i).

The fact that some commentators have interpreted qanitun here as "those who stand as witnesses to His Oneness" [^1] is, in fact, an expression of one of the instances of obedience, for testifying to the Oneness of Allah is a form of obeying Him.

Since the series of preceding and succeeding verses weave together the issues of the Origin (Mabda') and the Resurrection (Ma'ad) like the warp and weft of a single fabric, the next verse returns to the issue of the Resurrection and says: "And He it is who originates the creation, then repeats it, and that is easier for Him!" (وَهُوَ الَّذِي يَبْدَأُ الْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُ وَهُوَ أَهْوَنُ عَلَيْهِ) [^2]. In this verse, the Quran proves the possibility of the Resurrection with the shortest of arguments.

[^1]: Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani, under the verse in question, quoted this statement from one of the earlier commentators.

[^2]: Fakhr Razi quotes the author of Tafsir al-Kashshaf that regarding the birth of Prophet Jesus without a father, Allah said: "It is easy for Me" (huwa 'alayya hayyin), and because 'alayya (for Me) is placed first, it implies exclusivity—meaning this task is easy only for Me and not for others. However, in the verse under discussion, He says: "It is easier for Him" (huwa ahwanu 'alayh), and no exclusivity is derived from it, indicating that anyone who can perform a task at the beginning also has the power to repeat it (reflect on this).

It says: You believe that the beginning of creation is from Him; why then should the repeat—which is easier—not be from Him?

The reason "repeating" is easier than "originating" is that in the beginning, there was nothing at all and Allah brought it into existence, but in the repeat, at least the original materials exist—some scattered within the earth's soil and some in the atmosphere. The only issue is that of organizing and shaping them.

However, it is necessary to note that the expression of "easy" and "difficult" is from the perspective of our human thought; otherwise, for a Being who is Infinite, there is no difference between "difficult" and "easy." Fundamentally, "difficult" and "easy" have meaning only where there is talk of a limited power that can handle one task well but another with struggle. But when the discussion is about Infinite Power, "difficult" and "easy" become meaningless.

In other words, lifting the mightiest mountains on earth is as easy for Allah as lifting a piece of straw! Perhaps for this reason, He immediately follows in the verse: "And for Allah is the highest description in the heavens and the earth" (وَلَهُ الْمَثَلُ الْأَعْلَىٰ فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ).

For every attribute of perfection you can imagine in the heavens and the earth regarding any being—whether knowledge, power, ownership, greatness of existence, or generosity—its most complete and perfect manifestation belongs to Allah. This is because everyone else possesses it in a limited form, while He possesses it infinitely; their attributes are accidental, while His attributes are essential, and He is the primary source of all perfections.

Even our words, which are mostly for expressing our daily purposes, cannot fully describe His attributes, just as we saw in the example of the term ahwan (easier).

The above sentence is similar to what appears in Surah Al-A'raf, verse 180: "To Allah belong the best names, so invoke Him by them" (وَلِلَّهِ الْأَسْمَاءُ الْحُسْنَىٰ فَادْعُوهُ بِهَا), and in Surah Ash-Shura, verse 11: "There is nothing like unto Him" (لَيْسَ كَمِثْلِهِ شَيْءٌ).

Finally, at the end of the verse, as an emphasis or as a proof, it says: "And He is the All-Mighty, the All-Wise" (وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ).

He is All-Mighty and invincible, yet despite His unlimited power, He does nothing without purpose; all His actions are according to wisdom.

After presenting another part of the proofs for Monotheism and the Resurrection in the previous verses, the text proceeds to mention a proof for the negation of polytheism by way of a parable, saying: "Allah sets forth for you a parable from yourselves" (ضَرَبَ لَكُمْ مَثَلًا مِنْ أَنْفُسِكُمْ).

The parable is this: if you possessed slaves and servants, "do you have among those whom your right hands possess any partners in the provision We have bestowed upon you?" (هَلْ لَكُمْ مِنْ مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَانُكُمْ مِنْ شُرَكَاءَ فِي مَا رَزَقْنَاكُمْ).

"Such that you are both entirely equal therein" (فَأَنْتُمْ فِيهِ سَوَاءٌ).

"Such that you fear them independently and without your permission to dispose of your wealth, just as you fear your free partners regarding your wealth or inheritance" (تَخَافُونَهُمْ كَخِيفَتِكُمْ أَنْفُسَكُمْ).

Or such that you are not willing to make any disposal of your wealth without their permission.

When you consider such a thing improper and wrong regarding your slaves—who are your "metaphorical property"—how can you imagine creatures, who are the "true property" of Allah, to be His partners? Or how can you count prophets like Jesus عليه السلام, or the angels of Allah, or creatures like the Jinn, or stone and wooden idols as partners to Allah? What an ugly and illogical judgment this is! "Metaphorical subjects" who might quickly be freed and stand in your rank (as this plan was laid out in Islam) never stand in the rank of their owner while in the state of being owned, and they have no right to interfere in his domain. How then have you chosen "true subjects"—whose essence and existence belong to Allah, and it is impossible for this dependence to be removed from them, and everything you have is from Him, and without Him you are nothing—as partners to Allah?

Some commentators have said that this verse refers to the words the polytheists of the Quraysh used to recite during the Hajj rituals while saying the Talbiyah, for they would say: "Labbayk, O Allah, You have no partner, except a partner who belongs to You; You own him and what he owns!" [^1] It is obvious that this occasion of revelation, like others, does not limit the meaning of the verse; in any case, the verse is a response to all polytheists, taking an argument from their own lives which revolved around slavery.

The expression "what We have provided for you" (مَا رَزَقْنَاكُمْ) points to the fact that you are neither the real owners of these slaves nor the real owners of your wealth, for all belongs to Allah. Yet, you are not willing to hand over your metaphorical wealth to your metaphorical subjects and consider them your partners, even though from a generative perspective, no impossibility arises, as the discussion revolves around social constructs.

However, the difference between Allah and His creatures is a generative and unchangeable difference, and to consider them His partners is absolutely impossible.

On the other hand, the worship of a being is either because of its greatness or because of the benefit or harm that reaches a human from it; but these fabricated deities have neither this nor that! [^1] And in the continuation of the verse, to emphasize the need for the utmost reflection on the content of this question, it says: "Thus do We explain the signs in detail for a people who use their intellect" (كَذَٰلِكَ نُفَصِّلُ الْآيَاتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ).

Yes, by mentioning clear examples from the core of your own lives, We recount the truths so that you may employ your thought, and at the very least, not attribute to the Lord of the worlds something you do not even accept for yourselves.

But these clear signs and these types of vivid parables are for those who possess intellect, not for the ignorant, desire-driven wrongdoers upon whose hearts veils of ignorance have fallen, and whose mental space has been darkened by superstitions and pagan prejudices. Therefore, in the next verse, it adds: "Nay, those who do wrong follow their own desires without knowledge, and they follow no logic" (بَلِ اتَّبَعَ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا أَهْوَاءَهُمْ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ).

Because of their actions, Allah has cast them into the valley of misguidance, "and who can guide those whom Allah has led astray?" (فَمَنْ يَهْدِي مَنْ أَضَلَّ اللَّهُ).

[^1]: Tafsir al-Mizan, Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, and Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn under the verses in question.

[^1]: Some commentators have another interpretation for the phrase "you fear them as you fear each other" (تَخَافُونَهُمْ كَخِيفَتِكُمْ أَنْفُسَكُمْ) in this context, the summary of which is that these deities have no power for you to fear them even as much as you fear one another, let alone fearing them more (but the interpretation we provided at the beginning of the verse seems closer).

The use of "those who do wrong" (zalamu) instead of "those who associate partners" (ashraku) points to the fact that "polytheism" itself is considered the greatest "wrong" (zulm). It is a wrong against the Creator because they have placed His creature on the same level as Him (and we know that zulm is to place something where it does not belong).

And it is a wrong against the creation of Allah, as they have barred them from the path of goodness and felicity, which is the path of Monotheism.

And it is a wrong against themselves, as they have squandered all the capital of their existence and wandered lost in the wilderness.

Furthermore, this expression is a prelude to the next sentence: if Allah has led them astray from the path of truth, it is because of their wrongdoing, just as we read in Surah Ibrahim, verse 27: "And Allah leads the wrongdoers astray" (وَيُضِلُّ اللَّهُ الظَّالِمِينَ).

And it is certain that those whom Allah leaves and abandons to themselves, "for them there will be no helpers" (وَمَا لَهُمْ مِنْ نَاصِرِينَ).

In this way, He clarifies the grim fate of this group. Why should it not be so, when they have committed the greatest of wrongs, disabled their intellect and thought, turned their backs on the sun of knowledge, and turned toward the darkness of desire? It is natural that Allah withdraws His success (tawfiq) from them and leaves them in darkness, and no helper remains for them.

Ayahs

فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفًا ۚ فِطْرَتَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّتِى فَطَرَ ٱلنَّاسَ عَلَيْهَا ۚ لَا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلدِّينُ ٱلْقَيِّمُ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٣٠)
So set your heart as a person of pure faith on this religion, the original nature endowed by Allah according to which He originated mankind (There is no altering Allah’s creation; that is the upright religion, but most people do not know.) (30)
۞ مُنِيبِينَ إِلَيْهِ وَٱتَّقُوهُ وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَلَا تَكُونُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (٣١)
—turning to Him in penitence, and be wary of Him, and maintain the prayer, and do not be one of the polytheists (31)
مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ فَرَّقُوا۟ دِينَهُمْ وَكَانُوا۟ شِيَعًا ۖ كُلُّ حِزْبٍۭ بِمَا لَدَيْهِمْ فَرِحُونَ (٣٢)
—those who split up their religion and became sects: each faction boasting about what it possessed. (32)

Commentary

Up to this point, we have had extensive discussions regarding Monotheism (Tawhid) and the knowledge of God through observing the order of creation, using it to prove a Source of knowledge and power beyond the world of nature, drawing from the monotheistic verses of this Surah.

Following that, in the first of the verses under discussion, the talk is of "Innate Monotheism" (Tawhid al-Fitri); that is, it pursues the same issue through the inner self, internal observation, and necessary and inseparable perception.

It says: "Set your face toward the pure and sincere religion of the Lord!" (فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفاً).

Because "this is the nature (Fitrah) upon which Allah has created mankind; there is no change in the creation of Allah" (فِطْرَةَ اللَّهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها لا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللَّهِ).

"This is the firm and upright religion" (ذلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ).

"But most people do not know" (وَ لكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَعْلَمُونَ).

"Wajh" (وجه) means "face," and here it refers to the internal face and the direction of the heart. Therefore, the intention is not merely a physical turning of the face, but a turning with one's entire being, as the face is the most important member of the body and its symbol.

"Aqim" (أقم) is derived from "Iqamah," meaning to make straight and upright, or to establish.

And "Hanif" (حنيف) is from the root "Hanaf," meaning to incline from falsehood toward truth and from crookedness toward straightness; the opposite is "Janaf," which means inclining from truth toward deviation.

Therefore, a "Hanif" religion is one that has inclined away from all crookedness, deviations, superstitions, and errors toward truth and correctness.

The sum of this sentence means: keep your attention constantly directed toward the religion that is free from any distortion or crookedness—the religion of Islam, the pure and sincere religion of Allah. [^1] The above verse emphasizes that the Hanif and pure religion, free from any polytheism, is the religion that Allah has created within the nature of all human beings. It is an eternal and unchangeable nature, even though many people do not pay attention to this reality.

The above verse expresses several truths:

  1. Not only the knowledge of God, but religion and faith in general, in all dimensions, is an innate (Fitri) matter. It must be so, because monotheistic studies tell us that there must be harmony between the system of "Creation" (Takwin) and "Legislation" (Tashri'). Whatever has been introduced in the Divine Law (Shar') certainly has a root in nature, and whatever is in the creation and essence of man will be a complement to the laws of the Shar'.

In other words: "Creation" and "Legislation" are two powerful arms that act harmoniously in all fields. It is impossible for there to be a call in the Shar' whose root is not in the depths of human nature, and it is impossible for something to be in the depths of human existence and the Shar' to oppose it.

Undoubtedly, the Shar' sets limits, conditions, and constraints to lead nature so that it does not fall into deviant paths, but it never fights against the original innate desire; rather, it guides it through legitimate means. Otherwise, a contradiction would arise between Legislation and Creation that is incompatible with the foundation of Monotheism.

To put it more clearly, Allah never performs contradictory acts such that His creative command says "do it" and His legislative command says "do not do it!"

  1. Religion exists in a pure and sincere form, free from any contamination, within the human soul, and deviations are accidental matters. Therefore, the duty of the Prophets is to remove these accidental matters and allow the original nature of man to flourish.

  2. The phrase لا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللَّهِ (there is no change in the creation of Allah) and the subsequent phrase ذلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ (that is the upright religion) are further emphases on the innate nature of religion and the impossibility of changing this Divine nature, even though many people, due to a lack of sufficient growth, are unable to perceive this reality.

It is also necessary to note that "Fitrah" (فطرة) is originally from the root "Fatara," meaning to split something lengthwise, and in such contexts, it is used to mean "creation," as if at the time of the creation of beings, the veil of non-existence is split and they become manifest.

In any case, from the first day a human steps into the world of existence, this Divine light is burning within his soul.

Numerous narrations mentioned in the commentary of this verse confirm what we have said above, which we will discuss in the "Key Points" section, in addition to other discussions we will have regarding the innate nature of Monotheism.

[^1]: The "Alif and Lam" in "Al-Din" is for "Ahd" (reference), referring to the same religion that the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) was commanded to convey.

In the next verse, it adds: This turning of yours toward the pure and innate Hanif religion is "while you are returning to your Lord" (مُنِيبِينَ إِلَيْهِ). The origin and foundation of your existence is based on Monotheism, and ultimately you must return to this same origin.

"Munibin" (منيبين) is from the root "Inabah," which originally means repeated returning, and here it refers to returning toward Allah and returning toward the monotheistic nature.

This means that whenever a factor arises that deviates a person from the principle of Monotheism in terms of belief or action, he must return to Him. No matter how many times this is repeated, there is no obstacle, until finally the foundations of nature become so strong and the obstacles and deterrents so weak and ineffective that you stand continuously in the front of Monotheism, becoming the perfect manifestation of "فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفاً."

It is noteworthy that "أقم وجهك" (set your face) is in the singular form, while "مُنِيبِينَ" (returning) is in the plural. This shows that although the first command is singular and addressed to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), in reality, all believers and Muslims are addressed.

Following the command of "Inabah" and returning, He gives the command of "Taqwa" (piety/God-wariness), which encompasses all Divine commands and prohibitions. He says: "And fear Him" (وَ اتَّقُوهُ). That is, fear disobeying His command.

Then, from among all commands, He emphasizes the subject of prayer (Salah), saying:

"And establish prayer" (وَ أَقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ).

Because prayer, in all its dimensions, is the most important program for fighting polytheism and the most effective means of strengthening the foundations of Monotheism and faith in Allah.

Therefore, from among all prohibitions, He also emphasizes "Shirk" (polytheism) and says: "And be not of the polytheists" (وَ لا تَكُونُوا مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ).

For polytheism is the greatest sin and the greatest of the major sins; Allah may forgive any sin, but He will never forgive polytheism, as we read in Surah An-Nisa, verse 48: إِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ وَ يَغْفِرُ ما دُونَ ذلِكَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ ("Indeed, Allah does not forgive that partners should be ascribed to Him, but He forgives what is less than that for whom He wills").

It is clear that the four commands mentioned in this verse are all emphases on the issue of Monotheism and its practical effects, including repentance and returning to Allah, piety, establishing prayer, and avoiding polytheism.

In the last verse under discussion, He describes one of the signs and consequences of polytheism in a short and meaningful phrase, saying: "Be not of the polytheists, of those who split up their religion and became sects" (مِنَ الَّذِينَ فَرَّقُوا دِينَهُمْ وَ كانُوا شِيَعاً). And surprisingly, despite all the contradictions and differences they had, "each sect is rejoicing in what it has!" (كُلُّ حِزْبٍ بِما لَدَيْهِمْ فَرِحُونَ).

Yes, one of the signs of polytheism is fragmentation and division, because different deities are the source of different methods and the origin of separations and dispersions—especially since polytheism is always accompanied by and born of whims, prejudice, arrogance, selfishness, and self-conceit. Therefore, unity and oneness are not possible except under the shadow of God-worship, reason, humility, and self-sacrifice.

Consequently, wherever we see conflict and fragmentation, we must know that a type of polytheism is dominant. This subject can be stated as a conclusion: the result of polytheism is the separation of ranks, contradiction, the wasting of forces, and ultimately weakness, humiliation, and inability.

As for why each group of deviants and polytheists is happy with the path they have chosen and considers it the truth, the reason is clear: the function of whims and desires is to beautify the heart's desires in the eyes of man. The result of this beautification is an ever-increasing attachment and joy for the path chosen, even if it is a detour and misguidance.

Whim-worship never allows a person to see the face of truth as it is, or to find a correct judgment free from loves and hates.

The Holy Quran says in Surah Fatir, verse 8: أَفَمَنْ زُيِّنَ لَهُ سُوءُ عَمَلِهِ فَرَآهُ حَسَناً ("Is he, then, to whom the evil of his conduct is made alluring so that he looks upon it as good [like one who walks the path of truth and sees realities as they are]?").

Key Points

1- Monotheism: A Powerful Inner Attraction

Undoubtedly, just as rational and logical reasons give direction to man, within his soul there also exist inclinations and attractions that sometimes consciously and sometimes unconsciously determine his direction.

Their existential philosophy is that in vital matters, man cannot always wait for reason and logic, because this would sometimes cause the suspension of vital goals. For example, if a human, for eating food or sexual intercourse, wanted to take inspiration from the logic of "the necessity of replacing what is depleted" and "the necessity of the continuity of the race through procreation" and move according to that, his species would have become extinct long ago. But the sexual instinct and attraction on one hand, and the appetite for nourishment on the other, inevitably pull him toward these goals. The more vital and universal the goals, the more powerful these attractions are!

However, it must be noted that these inclinations and attractions are of two types: some are unconscious, meaning they do not need the mediation of reason and consciousness, just as an animal is attracted to food and the opposite sex without needing to think.

But sometimes its effect is conscious; that is, this inner attraction affects reason and thought and compels it to choose a path.

Usually, the first type is called "instinct" (Gharizah) and the second type is called "nature" (Fitrah) [Note this carefully].

God-seeking and God-worship exist as a nature (Fitrah) within the souls of all human beings.

Some here might consider this statement merely a claim made by the world's God-worshippers, but we have various evidences that clarify the innate nature of "God-seeking," and indeed religion in all its principles:

  1. The persistence of religious belief and faith in God throughout the eventful history of mankind is itself a sign of its innate nature. For if it were a habit, it would neither be universal and general, nor permanent and eternal. This universality and eternity are proof that it has an innate root.

Great historians say: as far as they have studied human history, and as far as they have access to pre-historic eras, they have never seen "irreligion" except as an exception in human societies.

Will Durant, the famous contemporary historian, says: "If we define religion as 'the worship of powers superior to nature,' we must consider from the very beginning of the discussion that some primitive tribes apparently had no religion"... then after mentioning examples for this subject, he continues: "Despite these, the examples mentioned are part of 'rare cases,' and the ancient belief that 'religion is a phenomenon that includes all human individuals' accords with the truth..."

Then he adds: "This case is considered by the philosopher as one of the basic cases of history and psychology. He is not satisfied with the idea that all religions are filled with vain and false matters, but rather pays attention to the fact that religion has accompanied human history since ancient times"... and at the end of his speech, he concludes with this meaningful question: "Where is the source of this piety located, which is in no way erased from the human heart?" [^1]

The same historian, in other researches he conducted regarding the existence of religion in pre-historic eras, says: "And if we do not imagine roots for religion in the pre-historic era, we can never recognize them in the historical era as they are." [^2]

Explorations regarding pre-historic humans through excavations also confirm these points, as the famous sociologist Samuel Koenig states in his book "Sociology" that: "The ancestors of today's humans (Neanderthals) certainly had a religion." Then, to prove this, he argues from the remains found in excavations that they buried their dead in a special manner and buried objects with them, which expresses their belief in the resurrection. [^3]

In any case, separating religion from human history is not something any researcher can accept.

  1. Objective observations in today's world show that despite all the efforts and struggles that some despotic regimes of the world have exerted to erase religion and religious traces through various means, they have not been able to uproot religion from the depths of these societies.

[^1]: The Story of Civilization, Vol. 1, pp. 87-89. [^2]: The Story of Civilization, Vol. 1, p. 156. [^3]: Sociology, p. 192.

We well know that the ruling party of the Soviet Union for more than 60 years, with continuous and non-stop propaganda and by taking help from all means of mass communication, tried to completely wash brains and hearts of religious beliefs. However, news that has leaked out from this closed environment, and which we recently read in the press, shows that not only were they unable to reach such a goal with all that propaganda and strictness, but recently more movement and attraction toward religious issues is seen in some Soviet republics, which has terrified the leaders of the ruling system. This shows that if one day the pressure and suffocation were removed, religion would quickly regain its place, and this is another witness to the innate nature of religion.

  1. Recent discoveries by psychoanalysts and psychologists regarding the dimensions of the human soul are another witness to this claim. They say: research into the dimensions of the human soul shows that one original dimension is the "religious dimension" or, in their terms, the "sacred" and "divine" dimension. Sometimes they have considered this religious dimension as the source of the other three dimensions: the dimension of "truth" (science), "goodness," and "beauty."

They say the basic and fundamental motives of the human soul are as follows:

  1. The "sense of truth," which is the source of various sciences and knowledge and the motive for continuous and persistent curiosity in knowing the world of existence.

  2. The "sense of goodness," which attracts man toward moral concepts such as justice, courage, sacrifice, and the like. Even if a person does not possess these qualities himself, he loves the heroes of these qualities, and this shows that the love for goodness is hidden in the roots of his soul.

  3. The "sense of beauty," which attracts man toward original arts, beauties, literature, and matters of taste, and sometimes becomes the source of transformations in the life of the individual and society.

  4. The "religious sense," meaning faith in a Transcendent Source and His worship and prayer.

In an article written by "Quainton" in this regard, we read: "Psychology, through searching in the unconscious soul of man—which was started by Freud and continued with the help of Adler and Jung—has reached a new world of hidden powers and modes of perception and knowledge beyond reason in the depths of the human soul, which may become one of the keys to solving the mystery of the 'religious sense.'

Although we are still far from a consensus on this, there is currently a current of thought that makes an increasing number of thinkers from various schools believe in a definition similar to what we bring below:

'The religious sense is one of the primary, constant, and natural elements of the human soul; its most original and essential part, and it cannot be applied to any other events, but rather... one of its springs gushes from the depths of the unconscious soul. Compared to the concepts of beauty, goodness, and truth, the "religious concept" or more correctly the "sacred concept" is a fourth category that possesses the same originality and independence as the other three concepts.'" [^1]

Also, in a translation and adaptation from the scholarly article by "Tanneguy de Quénétain," we read: "Just as it is one of the advantages of the present age to discover a fourth dimension in the world of nature called time or space-time, which is distinct from the three spatial dimensions and yet encompasses those three, so too in this age, parallel to the three concepts of beauty, goodness, and truth, a fourth category—the sacred or divine—which is in fact the fourth dimension of the human soul, has been rediscovered. In this position as well, this fourth spiritual dimension is separate from the other three concepts, and it may have been the source of the production of the other three dimensions!" [^2]

  1. Man's taking refuge in hardships and difficulties in a mysterious supernatural power and requesting the solution of problems and the subsiding of the harsh storms of life from His threshold is also another witness to the originality of this inner attraction and innate inspiration. Combined with the other evidences we mentioned, it can make us aware of the existence of such a powerful pull toward God within our being.

Of course, some might consider this attention a reaction to the religious suggestions and propaganda of the environment that we have been and are under the influence of throughout our lives.

But the universality of this phenomenon in all humans, and even in those who usually do not deal with religious issues, shows that it has a root deeper than this hypothesis—a root that is hidden in the depths of human existence and is not the product of propaganda and suggestion.

  1. In human life, events are seen that cannot be interpreted except through the originality of the religious sense: we see humans who lovingly sacrifice all their material possibilities for religious emotions, and pour all they have with unique self-sacrifice at the feet of their religion, and even give their lives for this cause.

The martyrs who drank the nectar of martyrdom with longing and love in the battlefields to advance Divine goals—examples of which are clearly and abundantly seen not only in the history of the Islamic Revolution but also in the history of other nations and peoples—are illustrators of this truth that the religious sense has a deep root in the human soul.

It might be objected that individuals like communists, who have never hidden their atheistic and anti-religious stance, also possess more or less such sacrifices in the way of preserving their school of thought and beliefs.

But by paying attention to one point, this objection is completely resolved: even communists, who apparently negate religion entirely and believe that religion belongs to past history and can never have a place in communist societies—yes, those very people—have unconsciously accepted another form of religion.

They look at their leaders with the same view that Egyptian idol-worshippers looked at their idols, and the long lines that form on many days beside Lenin's tomb for visitation is another proof of this subject.

They often consider the principles of Marxism as "indisputable" and "sacred" like heavenly revelation, and they imagine Marx and Lenin as innocents free from error and mistake, and even consider revision in these principles an unforgivable sin, and address opponents with the same religious term "apostate" (murtad). In this way, they have accepted many religious concepts, ceremonies, and beliefs, except that it is a type of religious thinking in a deviant form!

2- The Nature (Fitrah) of God-Knowledge in Islamic Hadiths:

Not only in the verses of the Quran, but also in Islamic hadiths, considerable discussions have been recorded regarding the innate nature of "Knowledge of Allah and Monotheism." In some, the emphasis is on "Monotheistic Nature," in some under the title of "Knowledge" (Ma'rifah), in others "Islamic Nature," and finally in some as "Wilayah" (Guardianship/Authority).

In a reliable hadith that the honorable traditionist "Kulayni" brought in Usul al-Kafi, he narrates from Hisham ibn Salim that he said: I asked Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام): What is meant by "فِطْرَتَ اللَّهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها"? He said: "It means Monotheism (Tawhid)." [^3]

Also in the same book al-Kafi, it is narrated from another companion of Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) that when he requested the interpretation of the verse from the Imam, the Imam said: "It is Islam." [^4]

In a similar hadith from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام), we read that in response to "Zurarah," one of his learned companions who had asked about the interpretation of the verse, he said: "He created them upon the knowledge of Him." [^5]

The famous hadith: "Every newborn is born upon the Fitrah (nature), until his parents are the ones who make him a Jew or a Christian," which is narrated from the Noble Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), also shows that every infant is born upon the nature of Islam and a religion free from polytheism, and colors such as Judaism and Christianity are deviations instilled in them through parents. [^6]

And finally, in a hadith also narrated in Usul al-Kafi from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) regarding the interpretation of this same verse, we read: "He said: It is the Wilayah." He said: "The meaning is the nature of Wilayah and the acceptance of the leadership of the Divine guardians." [^7]

In the first sermon of Nahj al-Balagha, it is also stated by the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), in a short and meaningful phrase: "Then He sent among them His messengers and sent His prophets to them in succession to demand from them the fulfillment of the covenant of His creation (Fitrah), and to remind them of His forgotten blessings, and to establish proof against them through preaching, and to uncover for them the hidden treasures of the intellects!"

According to the above narrations, not only "Knowledge of Allah," but the whole of Islam is placed in a compressed form within the human nature—from Monotheism to the leadership of Divine leaders and the true successors of the Prophet, and even the branches of the laws.

Therefore, according to the expression used in Nahj al-Balagha, the work of the Prophets is to make natures flourish, to remind of forgotten Divine blessings—including the monotheistic nature—and to extract the treasures of knowledge that are hidden and concealed within the soul and thought of humans.

It is interesting to note that the Holy Quran, in several verses, mentions hardships, difficulties, and painful events that occur in human life as "grounds for the flourishing of the religious sense." Among them, it says: فَإِذا رَكِبُوا فِي الْفُلْكِ دَعَوُا اللَّهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ فَلَمَّا نَجَّاهُمْ إِلَى الْبَرِّ إِذا هُمْ يُشْرِكُونَ ("When they embark on a ship [and are caught in terrifying difficulties in the middle of the sea], they call upon Allah, being sincere to Him in religion; but when He brings them safe to land, behold, they associate others with Him!") (Al-Ankabut: 65).

Of course, in this regard, under the subsequent verses in this same Surah—which are not unlike the verse of Surah Al-Ankabut—we will speak again, God willing.

Ayahs

وَإِذَا مَسَّ ٱلنَّاسَ ضُرٌّ دَعَوْا۟ رَبَّهُم مُّنِيبِينَ إِلَيْهِ ثُمَّ إِذَآ أَذَاقَهُم مِّنْهُ رَحْمَةً إِذَا فَرِيقٌ مِّنْهُم بِرَبِّهِمْ يُشْرِكُونَ (٣٣)
When distress befalls people, they supplicate their Lord, turning to Him in penitence. Then, when He lets them taste His mercy, behold, a part of them ascribe partners to their Lord, (33)
لِيَكْفُرُوا۟ بِمَآ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمْ ۚ فَتَمَتَّعُوا۟ فَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ (٣٤)
being ungrateful toward what We have given them. So let them enjoy. Soon they will know! (34)
أَمْ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَـٰنًا فَهُوَ يَتَكَلَّمُ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يُشْرِكُونَ (٣٥)
Have We sent down to them any authority which might assert what they associate with Him? (35)
