وَإِذَآ أَذَقْنَا ٱلنَّاسَ رَحْمَةً فَرِحُوا۟ بِهَا ۖ وَإِن تُصِبْهُمْ سَيِّئَةٌۢ بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ إِذَا هُمْ يَقْنَطُونَ (٣٦)
When We let people taste [Our] mercy, they boast about it; but should an ill visit them because of what their hands have sent ahead, behold, they become despondent! (36)

Commentary

The first verse under discussion is, in fact, an argument and an emphasis on the previous discussion regarding the primordial nature (fitra) of monotheism and the blossoming of this Divine light during hardships and difficulties.

It states: "And when adversity touches the people, they call upon their Lord, turning in repentance to Him" (وَ إِذا مَسَّ النَّاسَ ضُرٌّ دَعَوْا رَبَّهُمْ مُنِيبِينَ إِلَيْهِ‌).

However, they are so lacking in capacity, short-sighted, and enslaved by prejudice and the blind imitation of their polytheistic ancestors that "then when He lets them taste mercy from Him, at once a party of them associate others with their Lord" (ثُمَّ إِذا أَذاقَهُمْ مِنْهُ رَحْمَةً إِذا فَرِيقٌ مِنْهُمْ بِرَبِّهِمْ يُشْرِكُونَ‌).

The expression massa al-nasa durrun (adversity touches the people) refers to a slight distress.

Similarly, the expression adhaqahum minhu rahmatan (He lets them taste mercy from Him) refers to attaining a slight blessing, as the term "tasting" in such contexts is applied to small and minor matters, especially with the mention of the words durr (adversity) and rahmah (mercy) in the indefinite form.

This means that some people are such that with a minor problem, they turn to "Allah" and the veils are lifted from their monotheistic nature; yet, with a minor blessing, they completely change course, becoming intoxicated and negligent, consigning everything to oblivion.

Of course, in the first instance, it speaks generally that all people are like this—remembering God in difficulties—because the existence of the monotheistic nature is universal.

However, in the second instance, regarding blessing, it mentions only a "party" who pursue the path of polytheism, because a group of Allah's servants remember Him both in difficulties and in blessings; the changes of life never make them negligent of the remembrance of the Truth.

The emphasis on munibina ilayh (turning in repentance to Him), considering the concept we previously mentioned for inabah—which comes from the root nawb, meaning to return repeatedly to something—is a subtle allusion to the fact that the foundation and basis in human nature is monotheism and the worship of God, while polytheism is an accidental matter. When one loses hope in the latter, they inevitably return toward faith and monotheism.

It is interesting that "mercy" in the above verse is attributed to Allah, but "adversity" and distress are not directly ascribed to Him. This is because many of our troubles and problems are the result of our own actions and sins, whereas all mercies return to Allah, whether directly or indirectly.

The word rabbahum (their Lord), mentioned twice in the verse, is an emphasis that man senses Divine Lordship and governance over his own existence if false teachings do not drive him toward polytheism.

It is also necessary to mention that the pronoun in minhu (from Him) refers to Allah and is an emphasis on the reality that all blessings are from His side. Many commentators, such as the authors of Al-Mizan, Al-Tibyan, and Abu al-Futuh al-Razi, have chosen this meaning. However, some others, like Fakhr al-Razi, have returned this pronoun to durr (adversity) and interpreted the verse as: "When Allah grants them mercy in exchange for [after] the harm and distress, a group becomes polytheists" (thus, min here would mean "in place of").

But it is clear that the first interpretation is more consistent with the apparent meaning of the verse.

In the next verse, as a threat to these low-capacity polytheists who forget Allah upon attaining blessings, it says: "Let them be ungrateful for what We have given them" and do whatever they can (لِيَكْفُرُوا بِما آتَيْناهُمْ‌).

"And enjoy yourselves [with these fleeting worldly blessings] as much as you can" (فَتَمَتَّعُوا).

"But soon you will know [the evil consequences of your deeds]" (فَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ‌) [1]. Although the addressees are the polytheists, it is not unlikely that the verse has a broad meaning encompassing all those who forget Allah when blessings arrive and occupy themselves solely with the enjoyment and utilization of these favors, forgetting the Bestower of blessings.

It is obvious that the use of the imperative form here is for the purpose of a threat.

In the following verse, to condemn this group of polytheists, the discourse is framed as an interrogation mixed with rebuke, saying: "Or have We sent down to them an authority, and it speaks of what they were associating with Him?" (أَمْ أَنْزَلْنا عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطاناً فَهو يَتَكَلَّمُ بِما كانُوا بِهِ يُشْرِكُونَ‌). The word am (or) here is for interrogation, and the interrogation carries a sense of denial and rebuke.

[1]: The lam in the phrase li-yakfuru is the lam of command, and this command is by way of threat. The phrase tamatta'u is another command which also carries the aspect of a threat. Although the first is in the third-person imperative and the second is in the second-person imperative, it is as if He initially considers them absent and then, to intensify the threat, He confronts them and addresses them directly. However, some commentators have considered the lam to be the lam of consequence (lam al-aqibah), meaning "the end result of their affair was ingratitude for Divine blessings," but the first meaning is more consistent with the end of the verse.

That is to say, following this path and tradition must either be due to the call of nature (fitra), the judgment of reason, or the command of Allah. However, their conscience and nature, which manifest in hardships and difficulties, cry out for monotheism. Reason also says one must seek the One who is the "Giver of Blessings."

What remains is the command of Allah, which is negated in this verse, stating that We have never given them such an instruction. Therefore, they rely on no acceptable principle for this belief of theirs.

Sultan means something that causes authority and victory, and here it means a firm and convincing proof.

The expression yatakallamu (it speaks) is a type of metaphorical expression used when a proof is so clear that we say: "This is an eloquent proof that speaks to man."

Some commentators have suggested the possibility that sultan here means a powerful angel, in which case "speaking" would be in its literal sense—meaning We did not send an angel as a messenger of polytheism to speak with them in this regard.

However, the first interpretation is clearer.

Finally, the last verse under discussion, which provides another depiction of the mindset and spirit of these ignorant, low-capacity individuals, says: "And when We let the people taste mercy, they rejoice in it; but if evil afflicts them for what their hands have put forth, at once they despair" (وَ إِذا أَذَقْنَا النَّاسَ رَحْمَةً فَرِحُوا بِها وَ إِنْ تُصِبْهُمْ سَيِّئَةٌ بِما قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ إِذا هُمْ يَقْنَطُونَ‌).

In contrast, true believers are those who are neither afflicted by arrogance and negligence during times of blessing, nor by despair and hopelessness during times of calamity. They consider the blessing to be from Allah and offer gratitude to His threshold, and they consider the calamity as a trial and test, or the result of their own actions, and they practice patience and turn toward His threshold.

While faithless individuals struggle between "arrogance" and "despair," people of faith are situated between "gratitude" and "patience."

Furthermore, it is clearly understood from this verse that at least a portion of the calamities and troubles that afflict man is the result of his own deeds and sins; Allah intends to warn them by this means, to purify them, and to bring them back to Himself.

It is also necessary to remind that the phrase farihu biha (they rejoice in it) here does not merely mean being happy with a blessing, but rather refers to joy coupled with arrogance and a type of intoxication and negligence—the same state that overcomes shallow individuals when they attain some wealth or status. Otherwise, joy coupled with gratitude and attention to Allah is not only not bad but has been commanded: "Say, 'In the bounty of Allah and in His mercy—in that let them rejoice'" (Yunus: 58).

The expression bi-ma qaddamat aydihim (for what their hands have put forth), which attributes sins to the hands, is because most of human actions are performed with the help of the hand, even though sins are also committed with the heart, eyes, or tongue. However, the prevalence of the hand's actions has led to this expression.

Here a question arises: Does this verse contradict verse 33 of this same Surah (two verses prior)? Because in this verse, it speaks of their despair during calamities, while the previous verse speaks of their turning to Allah during the emergence of problems and hardships. In short, that one speaks of hope and this one speaks of despair?

However, paying attention to one point clarifies the answer to this question:

In the previous verse, the discussion was about the issue of durr (adversity), meaning harmful events like storms, earthquakes, and other hardships, during which all people—monotheists and polytheists alike—remember Allah, and this is one of the signs of the monotheistic nature.

But in the verse under discussion, the talk is about the repercussions of human sins and the despair resulting from them. For some individuals are such that if they perform a good deed, they become arrogant and consider themselves immune from Divine punishment; and when they perform an evil deed and its reaction afflicts them, despair of Allah's mercy surrounds their entire being. Both that "vanity" and arrogance are blameworthy, and so is this "despair and hopelessness" regarding Allah's mercy.

Therefore, each of the two verses raises a point that is distinct from the other.

Ayahs

أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَبْسُطُ ٱلرِّزْقَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ وَيَقْدِرُ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ (٣٧)
Do they not see that Allah expands the provision for whomever He wishes, and tightens it? There are indeed signs in that for a people who have faith. (37)
فَـَٔاتِ ذَا ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ حَقَّهُۥ وَٱلْمِسْكِينَ وَٱبْنَ ٱلسَّبِيلِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ لِّلَّذِينَ يُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ (٣٨)
Give the relative his due, and the needy and the traveller [as well]. That is better for those who seek Allah’s pleasure, and it is they who are the felicitous. (38)
وَمَآ ءَاتَيْتُم مِّن رِّبًا لِّيَرْبُوَا۟ فِىٓ أَمْوَٰلِ ٱلنَّاسِ فَلَا يَرْبُوا۟ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَمَآ ءَاتَيْتُم مِّن زَكَوٰةٍ تُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَ ٱللَّهِ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُضْعِفُونَ (٣٩)
What you give in usury in order that it may increase people’s wealth does not increase with Allah. But what you pay as zakat seeking Allah’s pleasure—it is they who will be given a manifold increase. (39)
ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُمْ ثُمَّ رَزَقَكُمْ ثُمَّ يُمِيتُكُمْ ثُمَّ يُحْيِيكُمْ ۖ هَلْ مِن شُرَكَآئِكُم مَّن يَفْعَلُ مِن ذَٰلِكُم مِّن شَىْءٍ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ وَتَعَـٰلَىٰ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (٤٠)
It is Allah who created you and then He provided for you, then He makes you die, then He will bring you to life. Is there anyone among your ‘partners’ who does anything of that kind? Immaculate is He and exalted above [having] any partners that they ascribe [to Him]! (40)

Commentary

The first verse under discussion continues to speak of the "Oneness of Lordship" (Tawhid al-Rububiyyah). In connection with the discussion in the preceding verses—where it was mentioned that some people of limited capacity become arrogant when a blessing comes their way and despair when faced with an affliction—it states:

"Do they not see that Allah extends provision for whom He wills and restricts [it]?" (أَ وَ لَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّ اللَّهَ يَبْسُطُ الرِّزْقَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ وَ يَقْدِرُ).

Neither should the appearance of blessings lead to arrogance, forgetfulness, and rebellion, nor should their withdrawal lead to hopelessness and despair; for the expansion and restriction of provision are in the hands of Allah. Sometimes He sees wisdom in the former, and sometimes in the latter.

It is true that the world is a world of causes, and those who are diligent and hardworking usually enjoy a greater share of provision, while those who are lazy, weak, and lack effort receive less. However, this is not a universal and permanent rule. For we sometimes see highly serious and capable individuals who, despite all their running, get nowhere; conversely, we sometimes observe less capable individuals for whom the doors of provision are opened from every side!

These exceptions are seemingly intended for Allah to show that, despite all the influence He has created within the world of causes, people should not lose themselves in the world of causes, nor should they forget that behind this system, there is another powerful Hand that directs it.

Sometimes He restricts things so much that no matter how hard a person strives or which door they knock on, they find all doors closed to them. And sometimes He makes things so easy that before they even approach a door, it opens before them!

This matter, which we have all encountered more or less in our lives, serves not only to combat the arrogance born of wealth and the despair born of poverty but also acts as proof that beyond our will and desire, another powerful Hand is at work.

Therefore, at the end of the verse, it says: "Indeed in that are signs for a people who believe" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ‌).

Some commentators have narrated a statement to this effect: A scholar was asked: Ma al-dalilu 'ala anna lil-'alami sani'an wahida?: "What proof do we have that the universe has a single Creator?" He replied: "By three proofs: the humiliation of the intelligent (dhull al-labib), the poverty of the refined and eloquent (faqr al-adib), and the illness of the physician (suqm al-tabib)!" [^1] Indeed, the existence of these exceptions is a sign that the matter is in the hands of Another. As we read in a famous Hadith from Ali عليه السلام:

’Araftu Allaha subhanahu bi-faskh al-ghara’im wa hall al-‘uqud wa naqd al-himam: "I came to know Allah, the Subhan, through the breaking of firm determinations, the dissolving of contracts, and the undoing of strong wills (when they fail to reach their goal)." [^2]

[^1]: Tafsir "Ruh al-Bayan," Vol. 7, p. 39, under the verse discussed. [^2]: "Nahj al-Balagha," Short Sayings, No. 250.

Since every blessing and gift brings with it duties and responsibilities, the next verse addresses the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, saying: "Since this is the case, give the relative his right, and [also] the needy and the traveler" (فَآتِ ذَا الْقُرْبى‌ حَقَّهُ وَ الْمِسْكِينَ وَ ابْنَ السَّبِيلِ‌).

In times of abundance, do not think that what you have belongs solely to you; rather, others also have a right in your wealth, including relatives and the destitute who are grounded by the severity of poverty, as well as honorable people who, far from home, have been stranded due to circumstances and are in need.

The expression "his right" (haqqahu) reflects the reality that they are partners in a person's wealth; if a person gives something, he is merely fulfilling their own right and holds no favor over them.

A group of commentators believe the addressee in this verse is exclusively the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and "the relatives" (dhi al-qurba) refers to his kin. In a famous narration from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri and others, it is stated: "When the above verse was revealed, the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله gifted Fadak to Fatima and handed it over to her" (Lamma nazalat hadhihi al-ayah 'ala al-Nabi (s) a'ta Fatima Fadakan wa sallamahu ilayha) [^1]. This same meaning has been narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام and Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام [^2]. This meaning also appears in a very detailed narration regarding the conversation of the Lady of Islam, Fatima al-Zahra عليه السلام, with Abu Bakr, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام [^3]. However, another group of commentators considers the address in this verse to be general, including the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and others. According to this interpretation, all people are duty-bound not to forget the rights of

[^1] and [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses discussed. [^3]: Tafsir Ali ibn Ibrahim (as cited in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 186).

their relatives and kin.

Of course, these two interpretations are not contradictory and can be reconciled: the concept of the verse is broad, and the Prophet and his relatives—especially Fatima al-Zahra عليه السلام—are its most perfect manifestation.

From this, it becomes clear that neither of the above interpretations contradicts the fact that this Surah is Meccan, because the concept of the verse is a comprehensive one that was to be acted upon in both Mecca and Medina. Even the granting of "Fadak" to "Fatima" عليه السلام based on this verse is entirely acceptable.

The only thing remaining here is the phrase "When this verse was revealed..." (Lamma nazalat hadhihi al-ayah...) in the narration of Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, which suggests that the granting of Fadak occurred after the revelation of the verse. However, if we take "Lamma" here to mean the "cause" rather than a specific "time," this problem is also resolved. The meaning of the narration would then be that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله granted Fadak to Fatima because of this divine command. Furthermore, sometimes verses of the Quran were revealed twice.

As for why only these three groups were mentioned among all needy and entitled individuals, it may be due to their importance; for the right of relatives is higher than any other right, and among the deprived and needy, the destitute (masakin) and the stranded travelers (ibn al-sabil) are the most in need.

Or it may be due to a point mentioned by Fakhr al-Razi here. He says: "The eight categories to whom Zakat must be paid apply when Zakat is obligatory. However, the three groups mentioned in the verse must be helped even if Zakat is not applicable. This is because some relatives are those whose maintenance (nafaqah) is obligatory upon a person, and the miskin is a deprived poor person whose life might be in danger if not helped,

and similarly, the ibn al-sabil might be in conditions where he would perish if help does not reach him. The order in which these three groups are mentioned in the verse is also proportionate to their order of importance." [^1] In any case, at the end of the verse, to encourage the doers of good and to state the condition for the acceptance of spending, He says: "That is best for those who desire the countenance of Allah" (ذلِكَ خَيْرٌ لِلَّذِينَ يُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَ اللَّهِ‌).

"And it is they who will be the successful" (وَ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُضْعِفُونَ‌).

They will be successful both in this world—as spending brings extraordinary blessings in this very life—and in the next world, where spending is one of the heaviest deeds on the Divine scale. Given that "the countenance of Allah" (Wajh Allah) does not mean a physical face for God—as He has no physical form—but rather refers to the Pure Essence of the Lord, this verse shows that the mere act of spending and paying the rights of relatives and other right-holders is not enough. What matters is that it be accompanied by sincerity and pure intention, free from any hypocrisy, showing off, reminding of favors, belittling, or expectation of reward.

It is also necessary to mention that, contrary to the statement of some commentators who have specified that spending for the sake of reaching Paradise is not an instance of "seeking the countenance of Allah," all actions that a person performs that have some connection to God—whether for His pleasure, to reach His reward, or to be saved from His punishment—are all instances of "seeking the countenance of Allah," although the highest and most perfect stage is to consider nothing but His servanthood and obedience.

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 16, p. 443

(1) Under the verses discussed.

In the next verse, in connection with the discussion of sincere spending, He points to two types of giving: one for the sake of God and the other for the purpose of attaining worldly wealth, saying: "And whatever you give for increase that it may increase within the wealth of people will not increase with Allah. But what you give in Zakat, desiring the countenance of Allah—those are the multipliers" (وَ ما آتَيْتُمْ مِنْ رِباً لِيَرْبُوَا فِي أَمْوالِ النَّاسِ فَلا يَرْبُوا عِنْدَ اللَّهِ وَ ما آتَيْتُمْ مِنْ زَكاةٍ تُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَ اللَّهِ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُضْعِفُونَ‌).

The meaning of the second sentence—giving Zakat and spending in the way of Allah, which results in great reward—is clear. However, regarding the first sentence, given that Riba originally means "increase," various interpretations have been offered.

The first interpretation, which is the clearest and most consistent with the concept of the verse and aligns with the narrations from the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, is that it refers to gifts that some people bring for others—especially the wealthy—with the intention of receiving a greater and better reward from them.

Naturally, in such gifts, neither the merit of the recipient nor priorities are considered; rather, all attention is focused on giving the gift where it can "catch" a larger amount! It is natural that such gifts, which lack the element of sincerity, are devoid of moral and spiritual value.

Therefore, the meaning of Riba in this verse is "gift and present," and the meaning of the phrase "that it may increase within the wealth of people" is to obtain a greater reward from people.

Undoubtedly, taking such a reward is not forbidden (haram), as there was no condition or contract involved, but it lacks spiritual and moral value. Hence, in several narrations from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام found in famous hadith sources, it is referred to as "permissible interest" (Riba Halal),

in contrast to "forbidden interest" (Riba Haram) in which a condition or contract is set.

In a hadith narrated in the book Tahdhib al-Ahkam from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of the above verse, we read: Huwa hadiyatuka ila al-rajuli tatlubu minhu al-thawaba afdala minha fa-dhalika riba yu’kal: "It refers to a gift you give to a man seeking a reward better than it; that is interest that may be consumed."

In another hadith from the same Imam عليه السلام, we read: "Interest is of two kinds: one is permissible (halal) and the other is forbidden (haram). As for the permissible, it is when a man gives a loan to his brother hoping that he will increase it and compensate him with more than he took without any condition between them. If he gives him more than he took without a condition between them, it is permissible for him, but he will have no reward from Allah for what he loaned, and that is His saying: 'it will not increase with Allah.' As for the forbidden, it is when a man gives a loan and stipulates that he return more than he took; this is the forbidden interest." [^1]

Another interpretation mentioned for the verse is that it refers to forbidden interest. According to this interpretation, the Quran wants to compare interest with sincere spending and say that although interest appears to be an increase in wealth, it is not an increase with the Lord. Real increase lies in spending in the way of Allah.

On this basis, they consider the verse a prelude to the issue of the prohibition of interest, which was first mentioned as a moral advice before the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله migration, and after the migration, its prohibition was implemented gradually in three other Surahs of the Quran (Surah al-Baqarah, Al-Imran, and al-Nisa). (We also alluded to this in Volume 2 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 270).

Of course, there is no contradiction between these two meanings, and the verse can be interpreted in a broad sense that includes both "permissible interest" and "forbidden interest," both being contrasted with spending in the way of Allah. However, the wording of the verse is more consistent with the first interpretation.

This is because the apparent meaning of the verse is that an action has been performed here that has no reward and is permissible, as it says: "this work does not bring an increase with Allah." This is consistent with permissible interest, which has neither reward nor sin, but is not something that triggers the wrath of the Lord; and as we said, the Islamic narrations also point to this.

It is also necessary to mention that mud'ifun, which is an active participle (ism fa'il), does not mean "those who multiply" here, but rather "those who possess a multiplied reward." In the Arabic language, the active participle sometimes means the possessor of something, such as musir, meaning "possessor of much wealth."

One should also not overlook that di'f and muda'af in the Arabic language do not strictly mean "twofold," but include "double" and "manifold." At the very least, in the case of the verse, it is tenfold (as the Quran says: "Whoever comes with a good deed will have ten times the like thereof"—al-An'am, 160).

In the case of a loan, it reaches eighteen times, as we read in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: ’Ala babi al-jannati maktubun al-qardu bi-thamaniyata 'ashara wa al-sadaqatu bi-'ashara:

[^1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 191.

"On the door of Paradise, it is written that the reward for a loan is eighteenfold and for charity is tenfold." [^1] And in the case of spending in the way of Allah, it sometimes reaches seven hundred times or more, as stated in verse 261 of Surah al-Baqarah.

In the last verse under discussion, it returns once again to the issue of the Origin and the Return (Resurrection), which was the fundamental theme in many verses of this Surah. It describes Allah with four attributes to serve both as an indication of Monotheism and the struggle against polytheism, and as a proof for the Resurrection, saying: "Allah is the one who created you, then provided for you, then will cause you to die, and then will give you life" (اللَّهُ الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ ثُمَّ رَزَقَكُمْ ثُمَّ يُمِيتُكُمْ ثُمَّ يُحْيِيكُمْ‌).

"Is there any of your 'partners' who does anything of that?" (هَلْ مِنْ شُرَكائِكُمْ مَنْ يَفْعَلُ مِنْ ذلِكُمْ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ).

"Exalted is He and high above what they associate with Him" (سُبْحانَهُ وَ تَعالى‌ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ‌).

It is certain that none of the polytheists believed that creation was carried out by idols, or that their provision was in the hands of idols, or the end of their lives; for they considered these fabricated deities as intermediaries between themselves and Allah and as intercessors, not as the Creator of the heavens and the earth and the Provider. Therefore, the answer to these questions is negative, and the interrogation is a rhetorical denial.

Another point that arises here is that they usually did not believe in life after death; how then does the Quran emphasize it in the final description of the Lord?

This expression may be because the issue of the Resurrection and life after death—as we have said in discussions on Resurrection—has an innate (fitri) aspect, and the Quran here relies not on their beliefs but on their innate nature.

Furthermore, it often happens that an eloquent speaker, when facing someone who denies a point, mentions it alongside other facts that the person accepts and emphasizes it decisively to make an impact and bring them down from the horse of denial.

Beyond all this, there is an inseparable link between Allah's power over the first life and life after death; given this logical relationship, both are mentioned in a single phrase.

In any case, the Quran says that when all these matters (creation, provision, death, and life) are in His hands, worship and adoration must also be exclusive to Him. With the phrase "Exalted is He and high above what they associate with Him," it expresses the truth that they have lowered the status of the Lord excessively by placing Him alongside idols and fabricated deities.

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 190.

Ayahs

ظَهَرَ ٱلْفَسَادُ فِى ٱلْبَرِّ وَٱلْبَحْرِ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِى ٱلنَّاسِ لِيُذِيقَهُم بَعْضَ ٱلَّذِى عَمِلُوا۟ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ (٤١)
Corruption has appeared in land and sea because of the doings of the people’s hands, that He may make them taste something of what they have done, so that they may come back. (41)
قُلْ سِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَٱنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلُ ۚ كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّشْرِكِينَ (٤٢)
Say, ‘Travel over the land, and see how was the fate of those who were before [you], most of whom were polytheists.’ (42)
فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ ٱلْقَيِّمِ مِن قَبْلِ أَن يَأْتِىَ يَوْمٌ لَّا مَرَدَّ لَهُۥ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَصَّدَّعُونَ (٤٣)
So set your heart on the upright religion, before there comes a day irrevocable from Allah. On that day they shall be split [into various groups]. (43)
مَن كَفَرَ فَعَلَيْهِ كُفْرُهُۥ ۖ وَمَنْ عَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَلِأَنفُسِهِمْ يَمْهَدُونَ (٤٤)
Whoever is faithless shall face the consequences of his faithlessness, and those who act righteously only prepare for their own souls, (44)
لِيَجْزِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ مِن فَضْلِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٤٥)
so that He may reward those who have faith and do righteous deeds out of His grace. Indeed, He does not like the faithless. (45)

Commentary

Corruption’s Source is the People’s Own Deeds!

In the preceding verses, the discussion centered on polytheism (shirk). We know that the root cause of all corruption is forgetting the principle of Monotheism (Tawhid) and turning toward polytheism. Therefore, in the verses under discussion, the text first mentions the emergence of corruption on earth due to the actions of people, stating: "Corruption has appeared throughout the land and sea because of what the hands of people have earned" (ظَهَرَ الْفَسادُ فِي الْبَرِّ وَ الْبَحْرِ بِما كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِي النَّاسِ‌).

"Allah intends to show them the reaction to their deeds and make them taste the consequence of some of the actions they have committed, so that perhaps they may wake up and return to the Truth!" (لِيُذِيقَهُمْ بَعْضَ الَّذِي عَمِلُوا لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ‌).

The above verse expresses a broad and expansive meaning regarding the connection between "corruption" and "sin." This is not exclusive to the land of Mecca and Hijaz, nor to the era of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله; rather, it is what is termed a "universal proposition" (qadhiyah haqiqiyyah) that explains the link between the "predicate" and the "subject." In other words, wherever corruption appears, it is a reflection of people's deeds, and it carries an educational purpose: so that people may taste the bitter flavor of the results of their actions and perhaps come to their senses.

Some say: This verse refers to the famine and drought that afflicted the polytheists of Mecca due to the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله imprecation; the rain ceased, the deserts became increasingly parched, and even utilizing the catch from the sea (the Red Sea) became difficult for them.

Even assuming this account is historically accurate, it is merely one instance (misdaq) and by no means limits the meaning of the verse regarding the relationship between corruption and sin—neither limiting it to that time and place, nor to drought and lack of rain.

From what we have stated, it becomes clear that many of the limited and localized interpretations cited by some commentators under this verse are unacceptable—such as the claim that "corruption on land" refers to the murder of Abel by Cain, or that "corruption at sea" refers to the usurpation of ships during the era of Moses and Khidr علیهما السلام.

Or the claim that "corruption of the sea and desert" refers to corrupt rulers who drag all these regions into depravity.

Of course, such individuals might be among the instances of corruption—those who gain dominance over people due to worldliness, compromise, and the people's submission to humiliation—but certainly, the entire concept of the verse is not confined to this.

A group of commentators has also debated the meaning of "corruption of the sea" (fasad al-bahr):

Some have said: Bahr (sea) refers to cities located by the sea, while others have said bahr refers to fertile regions rich in orchards and agriculture.

We see no reason for such forced interpretations (takalluf), as bahr has a well-known meaning, which is the sea. Corruption within it could manifest as a scarcity of marine resources or the insecurities and wars that occur upon the seas.

In a tradition from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "The life of sea creatures depends on rain; when rain is withheld, corruption appears in both the sea and the land, and that happens when sins and acts of disobedience increase!" [1] Of course, what is mentioned in this narration is a clear instance of corruption. Furthermore, the connection between rainfall and the life of marine animals mentioned in this hadith is a matter precisely proven by experience: whenever it rains less, fish in the sea decrease. We have even heard from some coastal dwellers saying: "The benefit of rain for the sea is greater than its benefit for the desert!" Regarding the relationship between corruption on land and sea and the sins of the people, we have other analyses that will follow, Allah willing, in the "Key Points" section.

In the following verse, so that people may see with their own eyes living evidence of the emergence of corruption on earth due to human sins, He commands "traveling through the land" and says to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Say: Travel through the earth and observe the state of previous nations; investigate their deeds and their fates, and see what was the end of those who came before you!" (قُلْ سِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَانْظُرُوا كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلُ‌).

[1]: Tafsir al-Qummi, as cited in Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 16, p. 210.

Look at their ruined palaces and castles; behold their plundered treasures; observe their once-powerful but now scattered populations; and finally, look at their broken graves and decayed bones.

See what became of their oppression, tyranny, sin, and polytheism.

See how, if they burned the nests of birds, the houses of these hunters were also brought to ruin.

Indeed, "most of them were polytheists" (كانَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ مُشْرِكِينَ‌).

And polytheism is the mother of all corruption and the cause of their destruction.

It is noteworthy that when the previous verses spoke of Allah's blessings, He first mentioned the creation of man and then his provision (اللَّهُ الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ ثُمَّ رَزَقَكُمْ‌). However, in the verses under discussion, when He speaks of divine punishment, He first points to the vanishing of blessings due to sin, and then to destruction and annihilation due to polytheism. This is because, in the act of giving, the first gift is creation followed by provision; but in the act of taking back, first comes the loss of blessings followed by destruction.

The expression "most of them were polytheists," considering this is a Meccan Surah and the Muslims were a minority at that time, perhaps suggests: do not fear the masses of polytheists, for Allah has destroyed vast groups of such people in the past. It is also a warning to these rebels to travel the earth and see with their own eyes the end of those who shared their ideology.

Since taking heed, waking up, and then returning to Allah is not always useful or effective, the next verse addresses the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, saying: "Set your face toward the straight and established religion (the religion of pure Monotheism) before a day comes from Allah which no one can avert and no one can suspend the divine program. On that day, people will be divided into groups and the ranks will be separated"—one group in Paradise and another in Hell (فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ الْقَيِّمِ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَأْتِيَ يَوْمٌ لا مَرَدَّ لَهُ مِنَ اللَّهِ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَصَّدَّعُونَ‌). [1] Describing the religion as qayyim (established/upright), given that qayyim means stable and sustaining, is in fact an indication of the reason for this constant attention to religion. That is, because the religion of Islam is a stable and straight path, and the sustainer of the system of people's material and spiritual lives, never deviate from it.

The fact that the address is directed to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is so that others may take account of their own affairs.

The term yassadda'un comes from the root sad', which originally means breaking or cracking a vessel, but it gradually came to be used for any kind of scattering or separation. Here, it refers to the separation of the ranks of the people of Paradise and the people of Hell, where each of these two ranks will also be divided into further ranks, corresponding to the hierarchy of heavenly degrees and hellish depths.

[1]: The word maradd in the phrase "لا مَرَدَّ لَهُ مِنَ اللَّهِ" is a mim-infinitive, used here in the sense of an active participle. Thus, the meaning becomes: "There is no averter of it from Allah." The pronoun "it" refers to "the Day." The overall concept of the sentence is: No one can turn that day back from Allah—meaning no one can prevent Allah's judgment, punishment, and reward by canceling that day. In short, neither does Allah break His promise so as to turn that day back, nor does anyone else have the power to do so; thus, the occurrence of that day is certain (reflect on this).

The next verse is, in fact, an explanation for this separation of ranks on the Day of Resurrection. It says: "Whoever disbelieves, his disbelief is to his own detriment," and its burden will haunt him (مَنْ كَفَرَ فَعَلَيْهِ كُفْرُهُ‌).

"And as for those who perform righteous deeds, they are preparing (divine reward) for their own souls" (وَ مَنْ عَمِلَ صالِحاً فَلِأَنْفُسِهِمْ يَمْهَدُونَ‌).

Yamhadun comes from the root mahd—as Raghib stated in Al-Mufradat—which originally means a cradle or a place prepared for a child. Subsequently, mahd and mihad were used in a broader sense to mean any prepared and ready place (where there is ultimate peace and comfort). The choice of this expression for the people of Paradise and the righteous believers is for this very reason.

In short, do not imagine that your faith, disbelief, or your ugly or beautiful deeds have any effect on Allah; it is you who will be made happy and joyful, or distressed and sorrowful by them.

It is interesting that regarding the disbelievers, He contents Himself with the phrase "Whoever disbelieves, his disbelief is to his own detriment," but regarding the believers, He explains in the following verse that they will not only see their deeds there, but Allah will grant them even more blessings worthy of His grace and generosity. He says: "The goal is that Allah may reward those who have believed and performed righteous deeds out of His grace" (لِيَجْزِيَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ‌). Certainly, this divine grace does not include the disbelievers, "for He does not love the disbelievers" (إِنَّهُ لا يُحِبُّ الْكافِرِينَ‌).

It is obvious that He will also treat them with justice and will not punish them more than they deserve, but they will not receive any grace or special bounty.

Key Points

1- The Relationship Between "Sin" and "Corruption"!

Undoubtedly, every wrong action affects the state of "society," and through it, the state of "individuals," causing a form of corruption in the social structure. Sin, wrongdoing, and law-breaking are like unhealthy and poisoned food which, whether we like it or not, will have an undesirable effect on the human body's system, and man will suffer its natural reaction.

"Lying" destroys trust. "Breach of trust" disrupts social relations. "Oppression" is always the source of further oppression. "Abuse of freedom" leads to dictatorship, and dictatorship to explosion. "Neglecting the rights of the deprived" creates hatred and enmity, and the accumulation of hatred and enmity destabilizes the foundations of society.

In short: every incorrect action, whether on a limited or broad scale, has an undesirable reaction, and this is one of the interpretations of the verse: "Corruption has appeared throughout the land and sea because of what the hands of people have earned" (this is the natural link between "sin" and "corruption").

However, it is understood from Islamic narrations that many sins, in addition to these, bring with them a series of sinister effects whose connection to those effects is, at least from a natural perspective, unknown.

For example, narrations state that "severing family ties" shortens life, "consuming forbidden wealth" darkens the heart, and the prevalence of adultery causes the destruction of humans and decreases provision... [1] We even read in a tradition from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: "Those who die because of sins are more than those who die by their natural appointed terms." [2] A similar meaning appears in another expression in the Holy Quran where it says: "And if the people of the cities had believed and feared Allah, We would have opened upon them blessings from the heaven and the earth; but they denied, so We seized them for what they used to earn!" (Al-A'raf, 96).

Thus, "corruption" in the verse under discussion is general, encompassing social corruption, calamities, and the withdrawal of blessings.

Another noteworthy point is that the above verse implies that one of the philosophies behind afflictions and calamities is their educational impact on humans. They must see the reaction to their deeds so they may wake from the sleep of heedlessness and return to purity and piety.

We do not say all evils and afflictions are of this kind, but we say at least a portion of them possesses such a philosophy, and of course, they have other philosophies which we have discussed in their proper place. [3]

2- The Philosophy of Traveling Through the Earth

The issue of traveling through the earth (sayr fil-ard) appears six times in the Holy Quran—in Surahs Al-Imran, Al-An'am, Al-Nahl, Al-Naml, Al-Ankabut, and Ar-Rum. One of these is for the purpose of studying the secrets of creation (Al-Ankabut, 20), and the other five times are for the purpose of taking heed from the painful and sinister ends of oppressive, tyrannical, and polluted nations.

The Quran grants special importance in educational matters to objective and sensory issues whose effects are completely tangible. Specifically, it commands Muslims to step out of their limited living environments and engage in travel and tourism across this vast world, to reflect on the actions and behaviors of other nations and their ends, and through this, to gather a valuable store of awareness and lessons.

Satanic powers in today's world, to expand the scope of their exploitation across the globe, have studied all countries, lands, and various nations, and have well-assessed their cultures, strengths, weaknesses, and material industries.

The Quran says: instead of these tyrants, you should travel through the earth, and instead of their satanic decisions, learn divine lessons.

Taking heed from the lives of others is more important and valuable than personal experience, because in personal experiences, a person must suffer losses to learn things, but in taking heed from the lives and experiences of others, a person gathers a precious provision without suffering any loss or damage.

The Quran's command regarding "traveling through the earth" aligns with the most complete methods that humanity has acquired today for its studies: after learning subjects in books, they take the students by the hand and lead them to travel the earth and study the objective evidence of what they have read.

Of course, today another type of travel under the title of "tourism" has become common by satanic civilizations to attract forbidden wealth, which often has deviant goals, such as the transfer of unhealthy cultures, debauchery, lust, licentiousness, and other unhealthy pastimes; this is destructive tourism.

Islam supports that type of tourism which is a means for transferring healthy cultures, accumulating experiences, becoming aware of the secrets of creation in the world of humanity and the world of nature, and taking lessons from the painful fate of corrupt and oppressive nations.

It is also relevant to mention that in Islam, another type of travel under the title of siyahat has been prohibited, as we read in a tradition: "There is no siyahat in Islam." [1] This refers to the life of those who would completely separate themselves from social life for their entire lives or a period of time, moving across the earth without any activity, living like monks and being a burden on society.

In other words, their work was "mobile monasticism," in contrast to the stationary monks who were isolated in monasteries and removed from society. Since Islam is opposed to monasticism and social isolation, it also condemns this type of siyahat.

[1]: Majma' al-Bahrayn, root s-y-h. In another tradition in the same book, it is narrated from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله that he said: "The siyahat of my nation is raiding and Jihad"—meaning if my nation wants to turn their backs on material life, why not go to Jihad? Why die uselessly in the deserts?

3- The Established Religion (Al-Din al-Qayyim)

In the above verses, the Prophet is commanded to direct his entire attention to the religion that is straight, stable, and firm—having no deviation toward crookedness and no instability in its foundations.

It is noteworthy that in other verses of the Holy Quran, other descriptions are given for the religion: In verse 105 of Surah Yunus, it is described as Hanif (a religion free from any deviation). In verse 3 of Surah Az-Zumar, it is described as Khalis (pure): "Unquestionably, for Allah is the pure religion." In verse 52 of Surah Al-Nahl, it is described as Wasib—a religion that is unchangeable and free from annihilation and decline (وَ لَهُ الدِّينُ واصِباً). And in verse 78 of Surah Al-Hajj, Islam is described as a religion free from any hardship (وَ ما جَعَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ مِنْ حَرَجٍ‌). And similar descriptions.

Each of these matters constitutes one of the dimensions of the religion of Islam, which are simultaneously necessary and inseparable from one another. Yes, such a religion must be chosen, one must strive to know it, and stand for its preservation until death.

4- There is No Return in the Resurrection

In the above verses regarding the Resurrection, we read: "A day which no one can avert from Allah"—it is a day that no one can hold back from Allah or prevent its occurrence, nor open a way to return to the world.

Similar expressions are found in other verses of the Quran; for instance, in verse 44 of Surah Ash-Shura, we read that when the oppressors see the painful divine punishment, they say: "Is there any way to return?" (هَلْ إِلى‌ مَرَدٍّ مِنْ سَبِيلٍ‌). And in verse 47 of Surah Ash-Shura, the Resurrection is also described as "a day which no one can avert from Allah."

The truth is that the universe has stages from which returning from a later stage to a previous one is never possible, and this is an unchangeable Sunnah of the Lord.

Is it ever possible for a child—whether born complete or incomplete—to return to the world of the womb? Is it possible for a fruit that has been separated from the tree—whether ripe or unripe—to join the branches once again?

The transition of man from this world to the next is the same; there is no way to return. This is a truth that makes one tremble and serves as a wake-up call to humanity.

Ayahs

وَمِنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦٓ أَن يُرْسِلَ ٱلرِّيَاحَ مُبَشِّرَٰتٍ وَلِيُذِيقَكُم مِّن رَّحْمَتِهِۦ وَلِتَجْرِىَ ٱلْفُلْكُ بِأَمْرِهِۦ وَلِتَبْتَغُوا۟ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ (٤٦)
And of His signs is that He sends the winds as bearers of good news and to let you taste of His mercy, and that the ships may sail by His command, and that you may seek of His bounty, and so that you may give [Him] thanks. (46)
وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ رُسُلًا إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِمْ فَجَآءُوهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ فَٱنتَقَمْنَا مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَجْرَمُوا۟ ۖ وَكَانَ حَقًّا عَلَيْنَا نَصْرُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٤٧)
Certainly We sent apostles to their people before you, and they brought them manifest proofs. Then We took vengeance upon those who were guilty, and it was a must for Us to help the faithful. (47)
ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى يُرْسِلُ ٱلرِّيَـٰحَ فَتُثِيرُ سَحَابًا فَيَبْسُطُهُۥ فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ كَيْفَ يَشَآءُ وَيَجْعَلُهُۥ كِسَفًا فَتَرَى ٱلْوَدْقَ يَخْرُجُ مِنْ خِلَـٰلِهِۦ ۖ فَإِذَآ أَصَابَ بِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦٓ إِذَا هُمْ يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ (٤٨)
It is Allah who sends the winds. Then they generate a cloud, then He spreads it as He wishes in the sky, and forms it into fragments, whereat you see the rain issuing from its midst. Then, when He strikes with it whomever of His servants that He wishes, behold, they rejoice; (48)
وَإِن كَانُوا۟ مِن قَبْلِ أَن يُنَزَّلَ عَلَيْهِم مِّن قَبْلِهِۦ لَمُبْلِسِينَ (٤٩)
and indeed they had been despondent earlier, before it was sent down upon them. (49)
فَٱنظُرْ إِلَىٰٓ ءَاثَـٰرِ رَحْمَتِ ٱللَّهِ كَيْفَ يُحْىِ ٱلْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَآ ۚ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ لَمُحْىِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ ۖ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٥٠)
So observe the effects of Allah’s mercy: how He revives the earth after its death! Indeed He is the reviver of the dead, and He has power over all things. (50)

Commentary

Behold the Effects of Allah’s Mercy!

We have mentioned that in this Surah, a significant portion of the proofs of Monotheism (Tawhid) and the signs of the Lord are presented across seven verses, each beginning with the phrase "وَ مِنْ آياتِهِ" (And among His signs). We previously studied six of these sections in succession, and the first verse under discussion is the seventh and final one among them.

Since the preceding verse spoke of faith and righteous deeds, the presentation of these Monotheistic proofs also serves as an emphasis upon that.

He says: "And among the signs of His greatness and power is that He sends the winds as glad tidings" (وَ مِنْ آياتِهِ أَنْ يُرْسِلَ الرِّياحَ مُبَشِّراتٍ‌).

They move ahead of the rain, carrying scattered pieces of clouds, joining them together, and driving them toward dry and thirsty lands. They cover the expanse of the sky and, by altering the atmospheric temperature, prepare the clouds to shed rain.

The importance of the arrival of these "bearers of glad tidings" might not be very clear to affluent city-dwellers; however, for thirsty desert-dwellers in need of drops of rain, as soon as the winds begin to move and displace the clouds, and as the specific scent of rain falling on plants elsewhere reaches their nostrils through the breeze, a spark of hope leaps in their hearts.

Although the verses of the Quran mostly emphasize the wind’s role as a herald of falling rain, the word "mubashshirat" (bearers of glad tidings) cannot be limited to that alone, for the winds carry many other glad tidings with them.

Winds moderate the heat and cold of the air.

Winds dissipate foul odors in the vast atmosphere and purify the air.

Winds reduce the pressure of the sun's heat on leaves and plants, preventing sunburn.

Winds bring the oxygen produced by tree leaves to humans and carry the carbon dioxide produced by human exhalation as a gift to the plants.

Winds pollinate many plants, joining the male and female elements in the world of vegetation.

Winds are a means for turning mills and a factor for winnowing harvests.

Winds move seeds from areas where they are abundant and, like a compassionate gardener, spread them across the entire wilderness.

And winds carry sailing ships with passengers and heavy cargo to various destinations; even today, when mechanical means have replaced wind power, the blowing of head-winds or tail-winds remains highly influential in the progress or slowing of ships.

Yes, they are heralds of glad tidings in various aspects.

Therefore, we read in the continuation of the verse: "That He may let you taste of His mercy, and that the ships may sail by His command, and that you may seek of His bounty, and perhaps you may be grateful" (وَ لِيُذِيقَكُمْ مِنْ رَحْمَتِهِ وَ لِتَجْرِيَ الْفُلْكُ بِأَمْرِهِ وَ لِتَبْتَغُوا مِنْ فَضْلِهِ وَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ‌).

Indeed, winds are both a means of producing abundant blessings in agriculture and livestock, and a means of transportation, and ultimately a cause for the flourishing of trade. The first is referred to by the phrase "لِيُذِيقَكُمْ مِنْ رَحْمَتِهِ" (that He may let you taste of His mercy), the second by "لِتَجْرِيَ الْفُلْكُ بِأَمْرِهِ" (that the ships may sail by His command), and the third by "لِتَبْتَغُوا مِنْ فَضْلِهِ" (that you may seek of His bounty).

It is interesting that all these "blessings" are born of "motion"—a motion in the air particles in the environment adjacent to the earth! Yet, the value of no blessing is truly known until it is taken away from man.

Until these winds and breezes stop, man does not realize what calamity would befall him. The stagnation of air makes life in the finest gardens like life in the dungeons of a prison; and if a breeze blows into the cells of solitary confinement, it makes them feel like the open air. Fundamentally, one of the factors of torture in prisons is the stagnation of their air.

Even on the surface of the oceans, if the winds were to stop and the waves were silenced, the life of sea creatures would be endangered due to the lack of oxygen, and the sea would turn into a terrifying, stagnant, and foul-smelling swamp.

Fakhr al-Razi says: The phrase "لِيُذِيقَكُمْ مِنْ رَحْمَتِهِ" (that He may let you taste of His mercy), considering that "tasting" is used for a "small amount," is an indication that the entire world and its blessings are but a small mercy, and the vast, all-encompassing Mercy of Allah is reserved for the next world.

The next verse speaks of the sending of Divine Messengers, while the verse following that returns once again to the blessing of the blowing winds. It is possible that the placement of this verse between two verses discussing the blessing of winds is parenthetical—as some commentators have said.

It is also possible that the mention of the issue of Prophethood alongside these discussions is to complete the matters related to the Origin (Mabda') and the Return (Ma'ad), which are repeatedly alluded to in this Surah—as others have suggested.

Furthermore, it may be a warning to all those who benefit from all these blessings yet remain ungrateful.

In any case, it says: "And We have certainly sent before you messengers to their people" (وَ لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ رُسُلًا إِلى‌ قَوْمِهِمْ‌).

"They brought them clear proofs of miracles and rational logic" (فَجاؤُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ‌).

A group believed, and a group rose in opposition, "but when the exhortations and warnings were of no avail, We took vengeance on the criminals!" (فَانْتَقَمْنا مِنَ الَّذِينَ أَجْرَمُوا).

And We aided the believers: "And it was ever a duty upon Us to aid the believers" (وَ كانَ حَقًّا عَلَيْنا نَصْرُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌).

The use of "كان" (was/has been), which indicates the deep-rooted nature of this Sunnah (tradition), and the use of "حق" (right/duty), followed by "علينا" (upon Us), which also expresses a duty, are considered successive emphases in this regard. Placing "حقا علينا" before "نصر المؤمنين" (a structure implying exclusivity) is another emphasis. Altogether, it means that We have certainly taken upon Ourselves the aiding of the believers, and without needing the help of anyone else, We will fulfill this promise of Ours.

This sentence also serves as a source of solace and encouragement for the Muslims who were at that time in Makkah under severe pressure from enemies who were superior in number and equipment.

Fundamentally, the fact that the enemies of God are drowned in pollution and sin is itself one of the factors for the victory and aid of the believers, because this very sin eventually uproots them, provides the means for their destruction by their own hands, and brings Divine vengeance upon them.

The next verse once again elaborates on the blessing of the blowing winds, saying: "Allah is He who sends the winds, so they stir up clouds" (اللَّهُ الَّذِي يُرْسِلُ الرِّياحَ فَتُثِيرُ سَحاباً).

"Then He spreads them in the sky as He wills" (فَيَبْسُطُهُ فِي السَّماءِ كَيْفَ يَشاءُ).

"And He makes them fragments, dense and piled upon one another" (وَ يَجْعَلُهُ كِسَفاً) [1]. "Then you see the rain-drops coming forth from their midst" (فَتَرَى الْوَدْقَ يَخْرُجُ مِنْ خِلالِهِ‌) [2]. Yes, one of the important roles during the falling of rain is assigned to the winds. It is they that carry the fragments of clouds from the seas toward the dry and thirsty lands, and it is they that are tasked with spreading the clouds across the sky, then densifying them, and thereafter cooling the environment of the clouds and preparing them for rain production.

The winds are like a knowledgeable and experienced shepherd who gathers the flock of sheep from around the wilderness at the right time, moves them along specific paths, and then prepares them for milking! The phrase "فَتَرَى الْوَدْقَ يَخْرُجُ مِنْ خِلالِهِ‌" (then you see the rain-drops and its small particles coming forth from the midst of the clouds) may indicate that the density of the clouds and the intensity of the winds are not to such an extent that they prevent the exit of small drops of rain from the cloud and their descent to the earth. Rather, these small particles, despite the storm and the clouds covering the sky, find their way through them toward the earth and are sprinkled gently upon the thirsty lands to saturate them well without causing destruction.

The wind and storm that sometimes uproot massive trees and move boulders allow the small and delicate drop of rain to pass through and settle on the ground.

It is also noteworthy that the fragmented nature of clouds—though not very perceptible to us on a cloudy day when clouds cover the entire sky—is perfectly clear and visible when we pass through the clouds by airplane or fly above them.

At the end of the verse, it adds: "Then when He causes it to fall on whom He wills of His servants, they rejoice" (فَإِذا أَصابَ بِهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ إِذا هُمْ يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ‌).

"Even though before it was sent down upon them, they were in despair" (وَ إِنْ كانُوا مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يُنَزَّلَ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ لَمُبْلِسِينَ‌) [1]. This despair and that joy are best understood by those whose life and livelihood, like the desert Arabs, are very closely linked to these drops of rain. While sometimes hopelessness and despair have cast a dark and heavy shadow over their souls, and the signs of thirst and parchedness have appeared in them, their livestock, and their cultivated lands, suddenly the winds that are the vanguard of the rain's descent begin to move—winds through which the scent of rain reaches their nostrils.

A few moments pass, the clouds spread in the sky, becoming thicker and more compressed, and then the rain begins. Hollows are filled with clear water, small and large streams overflow with this heavenly banquet, life and vitality sprout in the dry lands and in the depths of the hearts of these desert-dwellers, the spark of hope shines in their hearts, and the dark clouds of despair and hopelessness are pushed aside.

The repetition of the word "قبل" (before) in the verse is apparently for emphasis. It says: moments before the rain—yes, moments before it—faces were sullen and expressions were distraught, but suddenly the rain falls and a smile of joy is etched upon the lips. What a weak creature is man, and what a Merciful God is He.

In Persian, too, we sometimes repeat the time for emphasis; we say: "Until yesterday—yes, until just yesterday—so-and-so was my friend, but now he has become a fierce enemy." The purpose of this repetition is to emphasize the change in human states.

In the final verse under discussion, the address is directed to the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, saying: "So look at the effects of Allah’s mercy, how He gives life to the earth after its death" (فَانْظُرْ إِلى‌ آثارِ رَحْمَتِ اللَّهِ كَيْفَ يُحْيِ الْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِها). The emphasis on the word "فانظر" (look/behold) indicates that the effects of Divine mercy in reviving dead lands through the descent of rain are so manifest that they appear to every human with a single glance, without the need for searching.

The expression "Allah’s mercy" regarding rain refers to its blessed effects from various aspects.

Rain irrigates dry lands and nurtures the seeds of plants.

Rain grants trees a new life and vitality.

Rain settles the dust in the air and makes the human environment healthy and clean.

Rain washes the plants, giving them freshness.

Rain makes the air humid and mild, and breathable for humans.

Rain seeps into the earth and after a while appears in the form of qanats and springs.

Rain sets in motion rivers and floods which, after being harnessed behind dams, produce electricity, light, brightness, and motion.

And finally, rain moderates both hot and cold weather; it reduces the heat and makes the cold bearable.

The expression "mercy" regarding rain is also reflected in other verses of the Quran:

Including verse 48 of Surah al-Furqan, and verse 63 of Surah al-Naml.

And also in verse 28 of Surah al-Shura, we read: وَ هُوَ الَّذِي يُنَزِّلُ الْغَيْثَ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما قَنَطُوا وَ يَنْشُرُ رَحْمَتَهُ: "And He is He who sends down the rain after they have despaired, and spreads His mercy."

Then, considering the connection that the Origin and the Return have in various matters, it adds at the end of the verse: "Indeed, He who gave life to the dead earth by the descent of rain is the Giver of life to the dead on the Day of Resurrection, and He is powerful over all things" (إِنَّ ذلِكَ لَمُحْيِ الْمَوْتى‌ وَ هُوَ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدِيرٌ).

The use of "محيى" (Giver of life) as a subject noun (ism fa'il) instead of a present tense verb, especially with the "lam" of emphasis, indicates the ultimate degree of certainty.

We have seen many times in the verses of the Quran that this Heavenly Book chooses the revival of the dead earth after the descent of rain as a witness to prove the issue of the Resurrection.

In Surah Qaf, verse 11, after mentioning the life of dead lands, it says: كَذلِكَ الْخُرُوجُ: "Thus is the Resurrection."

A similar expression appears in verse 9 of Surah Fatir, which says: كَذلِكَ النُّشُورُ (Thus is the rising in the Resurrection).

In fact, the law of life and death is similar everywhere: He who revives the dead earth with a few drops of rain and creates excitement, stir, and movement in it—and this work is repeated every year and sometimes every day—has the power to revive humans after death as well. Everywhere, death is in His hand and life is by His command.

It is true that apparently the dead earth itself does not come to life, but rather the seeds of plants within the earth are nurtured; however, we know that these small seeds absorb a massive amount of the earth's components into their bodies and transform dead matter into living organisms, and even the decomposed particles of these plants again grant strength and power for life to the earth.

In truth, the deniers of the Resurrection had no proof for their claim other than considering it "unlikely," and the Holy Quran utilizes these living examples to shatter their sense of improbability.

[1]: "Kisaf" is the plural of "kisfah," meaning "piece" or "fragment," and here it apparently refers to pieces of clouds that pile up on one another, giving it density and intensity, and this occurs when the clouds are ready for the descent of rain.

[2]: "Wadq" is sometimes used for small, mist-like particles of water that are scattered in the sky during the descent of rain, and it is also applied to scattered drops of rain.

[1]: "Mublis" is from the root "iblas," meaning despair and hopelessness.

Ayahs

وَلَئِنْ أَرْسَلْنَا رِيحًا فَرَأَوْهُ مُصْفَرًّا لَّظَلُّوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ يَكْفُرُونَ (٥١)
And if We send a wind and they see it turn yellow, they will surely become ungrateful after that. (51)
فَإِنَّكَ لَا تُسْمِعُ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ وَلَا تُسْمِعُ ٱلصُّمَّ ٱلدُّعَآءَ إِذَا وَلَّوْا۟ مُدْبِرِينَ (٥٢)
Indeed you cannot make the dead hear, nor can you make the deaf hear the call when they turn their backs [upon you], (52)
وَمَآ أَنتَ بِهَـٰدِ ٱلْعُمْىِ عَن ضَلَـٰلَتِهِمْ ۖ إِن تُسْمِعُ إِلَّا مَن يُؤْمِنُ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا فَهُم مُّسْلِمُونَ (٥٣)
nor can you lead the blind out of their error. You can make only those hear who have faith in Our signs and have submitted. (53)
۞ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُم مِّن ضَعْفٍ ثُمَّ جَعَلَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ضَعْفٍ قُوَّةً ثُمَّ جَعَلَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ قُوَّةٍ ضَعْفًا وَشَيْبَةً ۚ يَخْلُقُ مَا يَشَآءُ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَلِيمُ ٱلْقَدِيرُ (٥٤)
It is Allah who created you from [a state of] weakness, then He gave you power after weakness. Then, after power, He ordained weakness and old age: He creates whatever He wishes, and He is the All-knowing, the All-powerful. (54)

Commentary

The Dead and the Deaf Do Not Hear Your Words!

Since the preceding verses spoke of the blessed winds that serve as heralds of the rains of mercy, the first verse under discussion points to harmful winds, stating: "And if We should send a wind [a scorching hot wind, or a cold and dry one, or one laced with toxins] and they see it [their agriculture and gardens] turned yellow, they would surely continue thereafter to be ungrateful" (وَلَئِنْ أَرْسَلْنَا رِيحًا فَرَأَوْهُ مُصْفَرًّا لَّظَلُّوا مِن بَعْدِهِ يَكْفُرُونَ).

They are weak individuals with little capacity; such that before the rain comes, they are despondent, and after it falls, they are exultant. Yet, if one day a toxic wind blows and their lives temporarily face hardships, their cries rise up and they begin to utter words of disbelief.

In contrast are the true believers, who are joyful and grateful for Allah's blessings and patient and steadfast during calamities and hardships. The fluctuations of material life never cause the slightest crack in their faith; unlike the blind-hearted who are weak in faith, they do not become believers with the blowing of one wind and disbelievers with the blowing of another.

The word "musfarran" (مُصْفَرًّا) is derived from the root "sufrah" (صُفْرَة), meaning the color yellow. According to most commentators, the pronoun in "ra'ahu" (رَأَوْهُ - they see it) refers to plants and trees which turn yellow and wither due to the blowing of harmful winds.

Some have also suggested that the pronoun refers to the clouds, as yellow-colored clouds are typically thin and usually do not carry rain, unlike black and dense clouds that produce rain.

Others consider the reference of the pronoun to be the "wind" (rih), because ordinary winds are colorless, while toxic, fire-like winds that occasionally carry desert dust are yellow and dark.

There is a fourth possibility: that "musfarr" means "empty." As Raghib has stated in Al-Mufradat, a vessel empty of content, a stomach empty of food, or veins emptied of blood are called "sifr" (صِفْر). Therefore, the expression here refers to winds that are empty of rain. (In this case, the pronoun in "ra'ahu" refers back to "rih") [Reflect on this].

However, the first interpretation is the most famous of all.

It is also noteworthy that here, beneficial and rain-bearing winds are mentioned in the plural form (riyah), but regarding harmful winds, the singular form (rih) is used. This indicates that most winds are beneficial, and the toxic wind is an exception that might blow once in a month or a year, whereas beneficial breezes are in motion throughout all days and nights.

Alternatively, it points to the fact that beneficial winds yield their effect when they come successively, while harmful gusts can sometimes leave their ill effect in a single instance.

The final point we deem necessary to mention regarding this verse is the difference between "yastabshirun" (they rejoice), used for beneficial winds in previous verses, and the phrase "la-zhallu min ba'dihi yakfurun" (they would surely continue thereafter to be ungrateful/disbelievers) in this verse.

This difference shows that they see all those great and successive blessings of Allah and rejoice, but if a calamity strikes for just once or for one day, they cry out so much and turn toward disbelief as if they will never let go of it.

They are exactly like those who are healthy for a lifetime and do not open their tongues in gratitude, but for one night when they burn with fever, they utter all sorts of disbelief and insults. Such is the state of ignorant people and those weak in faith.

We have had other discussions on this topic under verse 35 of this same Surah, verses 9 and 10 of Surah Hud, and verse 11 of Surah Al-Hajj.

In the following two verses, in connection with the discussion in the previous verse, people are divided into four groups: The group of the dead—those who perceive no truth even though they are outwardly alive. The group of the deaf—who have no readiness to hear the word of truth. The group who are deprived of seeing the face of truth. And finally, the group of true believers—who have knowing hearts, hearing ears, and seeing eyes.

First, He says: "So indeed, you cannot make the dead hear" (فَإِنَّكَ لَا تُسْمِعُ الْمَوْتَىٰ). Your words of truth cannot reach the ears of the dead, and your advice has no effect on the hearts of those whose hearts are dead.

And also: "nor can you make the deaf hear the call when they turn their backs, retreating" (وَلَا تُسْمِعُ الصُّمَّ الدُّعَاءَ إِذَا وَلَّوْا مُدْبِرِينَ).

And likewise: "And you cannot guide the blind from their straying" (وَمَا أَنتَ بِهَادِ الْعُمْيِ عَن ضَلَالَتِهِمْ). "You can only make hear those who believe in Our verses so they are Muslims [submitting to the truth]" (إِن تُسْمِعُ إِلَّا مَن يُؤْمِنُ بِآيَاتِنَا فَهُم مُّسْلِمُونَ).

As we have said before, the Quran recognizes a higher type of life, death, sight, and hearing beyond the material and physical ones, which is the primary root of human felicity or misery.

Its evaluation of these matters is not material or physical, but spiritual and human.

The first condition for perceiving the truth is having a ready and receptive heart, a seeing eye, and a hearing ear; otherwise, even if all the Prophets and Saints gathered and recited all the divine signs to someone who has lost the sense of discernment and perception of truth due to excessive sin, stubbornness, and malice, it would have no effect on him! If the Quran mentions only two of the external senses plus internal perception, it is because the vast majority of human knowledge is acquired either through these two senses (sight and hearing) or through intuitive perceptions and intellectual analysis.

Interestingly, the three stages mentioned in the above verses are three different stages of deviation and lack of perception of truth, starting from the most severe and ending with the mildest.

The first stage is "heart-deadness," expressed as "mawta" (the dead), where there is no way for any influence to penetrate them.

The second stage is the stage of deafness, especially the deaf who have turned their backs and are fleeing, where even loud shouts that might affect them when they are close become ineffective here. Of course, this group is not like the dead; sometimes it is possible to convey a point to them through signs and gestures, but we know that many truths cannot be understood through gestures, especially when they are turning away and moving further.

The third stage is blindness. Of course, living with a blind person is much easier than living with the deaf or the dead; they at least have hearing ears and many concepts can be explained to them, "but when was hearing ever like seeing?" Furthermore, merely explaining matters is not enough; even if a blind person is told to move to the right or left, following this instruction is not easy and sometimes a slight error in measurement leads to falling into a precipice!

In the detailed discussion we had under verses 80 and 81 of Surah Al-Naml, while analyzing the reality of life and death in the Quran, we addressed a weak objection raised by some Wahhabis. They use these verses and similar ones to deny seeking intercession (tawassul) from the Prophet and Imams, saying: the dead (even the Prophet!) understand absolutely nothing.

However, we proved there that humans—especially the great leaders and martyrs—possess a type of Purgatorial life (Barzakh) after death, and many proofs from the Quran and Hadith testify to this. In this Purgatorial life, they have a perception and vision broader than in worldly life (for more explanation, refer to Volume 15 under the verses mentioned above).

And here we must add this sentence: all Muslims always address the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله in their prayers during the Tashahhud and send peace upon him with the phrase: As-salamu 'alayka ayyuha-n-Nabiyyu wa rahmatullahi wa barakatuh (Peace be upon you, O Prophet, and the mercy of Allah and His blessings). We know that a real address—not a metaphorical one—is necessarily to someone who hears and perceives. Therefore, greeting the Prophet in the form of an address from far and near is proof that his holy soul hears all these greetings, and there is no reason for us to interpret these addresses as metaphorical.

In the last verse under discussion, He points to another proof of Monotheism (Tawhid), which is the proof of "poverty and richness" (need and self-sufficiency), completing the discussions on Monotheism found throughout this Surah. He says: "Allah is He who created you from weakness, then He made after weakness strength, then He made after strength weakness and white hair" (اللَّهُ الَّذِي خَلَقَكُم مِّن ضَعْفٍ ثُمَّ جَعَلَ مِن بَعْدِ ضَعْفٍ قُوَّةً ثُمَّ جَعَلَ مِن بَعْدِ قُوَّةٍ ضَعْفًا وَشَيْبَةً). Indeed, "He creates what He wills, and He is the All-Knowing, the All-Powerful" (يَخْلُقُ مَا يَشَاءُ وَهُوَ الْعَلِيمُ الْقَدِيرُ).

In the beginning, you were so weak and powerless that you did not even have the power to drive away a fly or hold your own saliva. This was physically; and intellectually, according to the verse "you knew nothing" (لا تعلمون شيئا), you did not even recognize the kind parents who were constantly watching over you.

But gradually you gained growth and power; you acquired a strong body, a powerful mind, a capable intellect, and a broad perception.

Yet, you could not maintain this power, and just like a person who has reached the peak from the slope of a high mountain, you began the descent on the other side, reaching once again the depths of the valley of physical and spiritual weakness.

These changes and ups and downs are the best proof of the truth that neither that strength was from you, nor that weakness; rather, both were from another source. This itself is a sign that the wheel of your existence is turned by another, and whatever you have is accidental.

This is what the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, pointed to in his luminous words, saying: "I recognized Allah, the Sublimely Purified, through the breaking of firm resolutions, the untying of knots, and the undoing of strong wills [and their failure]" [1]. From these changes, I understood that the primary power is in the hand of another, and we have nothing of our own except what He grants us.

Interestingly, regarding the second weakness that occurs for man, He adds the word "shaybah" (old age/white hair), but in the first weakness, He does not mention "childhood."

This expression may indicate that the weakness of old age is more painful, because firstly, it moves toward death and annihilation, unlike the weakness of childhood; and secondly, the expectations people have of the elderly and experienced are never expected of children, even though their weakness and inability are sometimes identical, and this is very much a cause for reflection.

This is the stage that brings rebellious and arrogant powerful ones to their knees and drags them into weakness, humiliation, and helplessness.

The final sentence of the verse, which refers to Allah's knowledge and power, is both a glad tiding and a warning: that Allah is aware of all your actions and intentions, and He also has the power to reward and punish you.

[1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, No. 250.

Ayahs

وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ ٱلسَّاعَةُ يُقْسِمُ ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ مَا لَبِثُوا۟ غَيْرَ سَاعَةٍ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ كَانُوا۟ يُؤْفَكُونَ (٥٥)
On the day when the Hour sets in, the guilty will swear that they had remained only for an hour. That is how they were used to lying [in the world]. (55)
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمَ وَٱلْإِيمَـٰنَ لَقَدْ لَبِثْتُمْ فِى كِتَـٰبِ ٱللَّهِ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْبَعْثِ ۖ فَهَـٰذَا يَوْمُ ٱلْبَعْثِ وَلَـٰكِنَّكُمْ كُنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٥٦)
But those who were given knowledge and faith will say, ‘Certainly you remained in Allah’s Book until the Day of Resurrection. This is the Day of Resurrection, but you did not know.’ (56)
فَيَوْمَئِذٍ لَّا يَنفَعُ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ مَعْذِرَتُهُمْ وَلَا هُمْ يُسْتَعْتَبُونَ (٥٧)
On that day, the excuses of the wrongdoers will not benefit them, nor will they be asked to propitiate [Allah]. (57)
وَلَقَدْ ضَرَبْنَا لِلنَّاسِ فِى هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانِ مِن كُلِّ مَثَلٍ ۚ وَلَئِن جِئْتَهُم بِـَٔايَةٍ لَّيَقُولَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِنْ أَنتُمْ إِلَّا مُبْطِلُونَ (٥٨)
Certainly we have drawn for mankind in this Quran every [kind of] parable. Indeed if you bring them a sign, the faithless will surely say, ‘You are nothing but fabricators!’ (58)
كَذَٰلِكَ يَطْبَعُ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٥٩)
Thus does Allah seal the hearts of those who do not know. (59)
فَٱصْبِرْ إِنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ ۖ وَلَا يَسْتَخِفَّنَّكَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُوقِنُونَ (٦٠)
So be patient! Allah’s promise is indeed true. And do not let yourself be upset by those who have no conviction. (60)

Commentary

The Day When Excuses Will Not Avail!

We have noted that in this Surah, discussions regarding the "Origin" (Mabda') and the "Resurrection" (Ma'ad) are integrated like the warp and weft of a fabric. In the verses under discussion, following the previous debates surrounding the Origin and Resurrection, the text returns once more to the issue of the Day of Judgment, visualizing another painful scene of the state of the criminals on that day.

It says: "On the day when the Hour is established, the criminals will swear that they remained [in the world of Barzakh] for no more than an hour!" (وَ يَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ يُقْسِمُ الْمُجْرِمُونَ ما لَبِثُوا غَيْرَ ساعَةٍ).

Indeed, "Thus were they deluded [from perceiving the truth] in the past as well!" (كَذلِكَ كانُوا يُؤْفَكُونَ‌).

The use of the term "Hour" (ساعة) for the Day of Resurrection—as we have previously pointed out—is either because the Resurrection will be established in a sudden moment, or because the accounting of the servants' deeds will be conducted with great speed, for Allah is swift in taking account. We know that "Hour" in the Arabic language refers to a small portion of time [^1].
