Although the above verse does not explicitly mention the location of this stay, and thus some have suggested it refers to the stay in this world—which is indeed nothing more than a fleeting moment—the subsequent verse provides clear evidence that the intended meaning is the stay in the world of Barzakh (the realm after death and before the Resurrection). This is because the phrase "You have remained according to the Decree of Allah until the Day of Resurrection" (لَقَدْ لَبِثْتُمْ فِي كِتابِ اللَّهِ إِلى‌ يَوْمِ الْبَعْثِ) marks the end of this period at the Resurrection, which is only applicable to the Barzakh (reflect upon this).

We also know that the Barzakh is not the same for everyone. One group experiences a conscious life in the world of Barzakh, while another group is like those who fall into a deep sleep, seemingly waking from their slumber on the Day of Resurrection, perceiving thousands of years as a mere hour! [^2] Two points remain here: First, how can the "criminals" swear such a false oath?

The answer is clear: they truly perceive the duration of Barzakh to have been very short because they were in a state similar to sleep. Did not the People of the Cave (Ashab al-Kahf), who were righteous believers, imagine upon waking from their very long sleep that they had slept for only a day or part of a day? Or consider one of the Prophets of Allah, whose story is mentioned in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 259; after he passed away and was brought back to life after a hundred years, did he not state that the interval between the two lives was a day or part of a day?

What is to prevent the criminals, given their specific state in Barzakh, from having such a misconception due to a lack of awareness?

Therefore, in the following verse, it will be mentioned that the knowledgeable believers say to them: "You are mistaken; you have remained in the world of Barzakh until the Day of Resurrection, and now is the Day of Resurrection!"

From this, the second point—the interpretation of the phrase "Thus were they deluded" (كَذلِكَ كانُوا يُؤْفَكُونَ‌)—becomes clear. "Ifk" (افك) originally means the distortion of the true face of reality and turning away from the truth. This group, due to their specific condition in Barzakh, remained far from the realities and cannot discern the duration of their stay there.

Given what we have explained, we see no need for the lengthy discussions by some commentators as to why the criminals would intentionally lie on the Day of Resurrection, for there is no evidence in the verse of an intentional lie at this stage.

Of course, in other verses of the Quran, examples of the criminals' lying on the Day of Resurrection are seen, the detailed answer to which we previously provided in Volume 5, page 187, under verse 23 of Surah Al-An'am. Regardless, that discussion is not related to the subject of these verses.

The next verse recounts the response of the knowledgeable believers to the words of the ignorant criminals who lack correct information about the state of Barzakh and the Resurrection. It says: "And those who were given knowledge and faith will say: 'You have remained, by the decree of Allah, until the Day of Resurrection [in the world of Barzakh], and this is the Day of Resurrection, but you did not know'" (وَ قالَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ وَ الْإِيمانَ لَقَدْ لَبِثْتُمْ فِي كِتابِ اللَّهِ إِلى‌ يَوْمِ الْبَعْثِ فَهذا يَوْمُ الْبَعْثِ وَ لكِنَّكُمْ كُنْتُمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَ‌).

The precedence of "knowledge" over "faith" is because knowledge is the foundation of faith.

The expression "in the Decree of Allah" (فِي كِتابِ اللَّهِ‌) may refer to the "Ontological Book" (the laws of creation), the "Divinely Revealed Books," or both. That is, by the ontological and legislative command of the Lord, it was ordained that you remain in Barzakh for such a duration and then be resurrected on the Day of Judgment [^1]. Regarding who is meant by "those who were given knowledge and faith":

Some commentators consider it a reference to the angels of Allah, who possess both knowledge and faith. Others consider it a reference to the knowledgeable believers, and the second meaning is more apparent.

The fact that some narrations interpret this as referring to the children of the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, and the Pure Imams علیهم السلام, is an instance of identifying the most prominent examples and does not limit the broad meaning of the verse.

It is also worth mentioning that some commentators believe the dialogue between these two groups regarding Barzakh—where one perceives it as an hour and the other is aware of its actual duration—stems from the fact that the first group, awaiting Divine punishment, wishes for it to be delayed as much as possible and thus considers the intervals, however long, to be insignificant. However, the second group, awaiting Paradise and eternal blessings, perceives these intervals as very long [^1].

In any case, when the criminals find themselves facing the painful realities of the Day of Resurrection, they attempt to offer excuses and repent. However, the Quran says: "On that Day, the excuses of the wrongdoers will not benefit them, nor will they be allowed to seek favor [through repentance]" (فَيَوْمَئِذٍ لا يَنْفَعُ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا مَعْذِرَتُهمْ وَ لا هُمْ يُسْتَعْتَبُونَ‌) [^2]. It is noteworthy that in some verses of the Quran, it is explicitly stated that the criminals are not permitted to offer excuses at all: "They will not be permitted to apologize" (Surah Al-Mursalat, 36). Yet here it says their excuses will not benefit them, which implies they do offer excuses, but to no effect. There is no contradiction between these verses, as the Resurrection has various stages. In some stages, they are not permitted to offer excuses or speak at all, and their mouths are sealed; only their hands, feet, limbs, and the earth upon which they sinned will recount their deeds. But in other stages, their tongues are loosened and they begin to offer excuses—but to what avail?

One of their excuses is to blame their sins on the leaders of disbelief and hypocrisy, saying to them: "If it were not for you, we would have been believers" (Surah Saba, verse 31).

But they will reply: "Did we avert you from guidance after it had come to you [and you saw it with open eyes]?" (Saba, 32).

And sometimes, in making excuses, they try to blame their deviation on Satan and reproach him for his whisperings. But Iblis will answer them: "Do not blame me, but blame yourselves!" (Ibrahim, 22). "I did not compel you to do anything; I only gave you a friendly invitation, and you accepted it!"

The next verse is, in fact, an allusion to all the topics discussed in this Surah. It says: "And We have certainly set forth for the people in this Quran every kind of example" (promises and threats, commands and prohibitions, glad tidings and warnings, signs in the horizons and within souls, proofs of the Origin and Resurrection, and news of the unseen—in short, We have provided an explanation for everything that might have a positive effect on human souls) (وَ لَقَدْ ضَرَبْنا لِلنَّاسِ فِي هذَا الْقُرْآنِ مِنْ كُلِّ مَثَلٍ‌). In truth, the Quran in general, and Surah Ar-Rum—which we are now in its final stages—in particular, is a collection of awakening issues for every class and group, and for every way of thinking and belief.

It is a collection of lessons, moral issues, practical programs, and matters of belief, utilized in every possible way to penetrate human thought and invite them to the path of salvation.

Yet, despite this, there is a group in whose dark and blackened hearts none of these matters have any effect. "Therefore, if you bring them any sign [of the truth], those who disbelieve will surely say: 'You are but followers of falsehood, and these are baseless matters!'" (وَ لَئِنْ جِئْتَهُمْ بِآيَةٍ لَيَقُولَنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِنْ أَنْتُمْ إِلَّا مُبْطِلُونَ‌).

The term "mubtilun" (followers of falsehood) is a comprehensive term that encompasses all the labels and unjust attributions the polytheists used: accusations of lying, sorcery, madness, superstitious myths, and legends—each of which is a face of falsehood. Indeed, they always accused the Prophets of Allah of one of these false matters to deceive pure-hearted people for a few days.

The addressee in "you" (انتم) may be the Prophet and the true believers, or it may be all supporters of the truth and all the Prophets and Divine leaders, for this group of stubborn disbelievers was opposed to all followers of this school of thought.

The next verse clearly explains the reason for this group's opposition. It says: This impenetrability, boundless stubbornness, and enmity toward every truth is because they have lost their sense of discernment and understanding due to the abundance of sin and obstinacy, and they understand nothing at all. Indeed, "Thus does Allah seal the hearts of those who do not know" (كَذلِكَ يَطْبَعُ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ قُلُوبِ الَّذِينَ لا يَعْلَمُونَ‌).

"Yatba'u" (يطبع) is from the root "tab'" (طبع), meaning to seal. it refers to a practice that was common in the past and remains so today: sometimes, to ensure something remains untouched and absolutely no tampering occurs, they close its door tightly and place a dough-like substance over the lock or knot and press a seal onto it. Obviously, opening it is impossible without breaking the seal, an act that would be immediately exposed.

The Quran uses this expressive metaphor as an allusion to impenetrable hearts and those who have completely lost their conscience, awareness, and sound reason, and for whose guidance there is no hope.

It is noteworthy that in the previous verses, knowledge was recognized as the foundation of faith, while in this verse, ignorance is the foundation of disbelief and the refusal to submit to the truth.

The final verse under discussion, which is the last verse of Surah Ar-Rum, gives two important commands and one great glad tiding to the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, to invite him to steadfastness in this continuous and persistent struggle against these ignorant stubborn ones and obstinate fools. First, it says: "Now that it is so, be patient and steadfast in the face of all events, all harassment, sabotage, and unjust accusations" (فَاصْبِرْ).

For patience, endurance, and steadfastness are the primary keys to victory.

And to make the Prophet more encouraged on this path, it adds: "Indeed, the promise of Allah is true" (إِنَّ وَعْدَ اللَّهِ حَقٌ‌).

He has promised you and the believers victory, succession on the earth, the triumph of Islam over disbelief, light over darkness, and knowledge over ignorance, and He will fulfill this promise.

The word "promise" (وعد) here refers to the repeated promises given in the Quran regarding the victory of the believers. For instance, in verse 47 of this very Surah, we read: "And it was a duty upon Us to help the believers" (وَ كانَ حَقًّا عَلَيْنا نَصْرُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌).

And in verse 51 of Surah Ghafir, it says: "Indeed, We will support Our messengers and those who believe during the life of this world and on the Day when the witnesses will stand" (إِنَّا لَنَنْصُرُ رُسُلَنا وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَ يَوْمَ يَقُومُ الْأَشْهادُ).

Also, verse 56 of Surah Al-Ma'idah says: "Indeed, the party of Allah—they are the victorious" (فَإِنَّ حِزْبَ اللَّهِ هُمُ الْغالِبُونَ‌).

The second command is to maintain control over one's nerves and preserve dignity and calm—not to be "unsettled" in this difficult and persistent struggle. It says: "And let them not disquiet you [make you angry or light-headed] who are not certain in faith" (وَ لا يَستَخِفَّنَّكَ الَّذِينَ لا يُوقِنُونَ‌).

Your duty is to have as much forbearance, endurance, and patience as possible, and to maintain the dignity befitting a leader in the face of such individuals.

"La yastakhiffannaka" (لا يستخفنك) is from the root "khiffat" (خفت), meaning lightness. That is, be so heavy and firm that these individuals cannot consider you light or shake you from your place. Stand firm and solid in your path, for they have no certainty, while you are the center of certainty and faith.

This Surah began with the promise of the believers' victory over their enemies and ends with the promise of victory as well, but it considers the primary condition for it to be patience and steadfastness.

O Lord! Grant us such patience and steadfastness that the storms of difficulties and harsh events never shake us from our place. O Allah! We seek refuge in Your Pure Essence from being among those whose hearts are not affected by sermons, advice, lessons, and warnings.

O God! The enemies are organized and united, armed with all kinds of satanic weapons; we seek from You victory over the external enemies and the internal Satan. Amen, O Lord of the Worlds.

End of Surah Ar-Rum and end of Volume 16 of Tafsir Nemouneh. Date: 21 Dhu al-Qi'dah 1403 (August 30, 1983).
